title: Status page + customer comms templates (failover / regional outage)
preview:
h1. Purpose
Provide consistent external communications during outages and failovers. These templates are written to be accurate even when details are still emerging.

h1. Principles
- Acknowledge impact and symptoms (errors/latency).
- Avoid speculation about root cause.
- State what we are doing next (investigating, mitigating, failing over).
- Provide the next update time.

h1. Status page template: incident start
*Title:* Increased error rates for Redwood Hosted API
*Body:* We are investigating elevated error rates and latency affecting a subset of requests to the Redwood Hosted API. We have identified a regional service degradation and are working to mitigate. Next update in 30 minutes.

h1. Status page template: failover initiated
*Title:* Regional degradation; traffic failover in progress
*Body:* We are performing a controlled traffic failover to a secondary region to mitigate ongoing elevated error rates. During this process, some requests may see transient errors or increased latency. Next update in 30 minutes.

h1. Status page template: failover complete
*Title:* Traffic routed to secondary region
*Body:* We have completed traffic failover to a secondary region and are monitoring service stability. Some users may continue to experience elevated latency while capacity stabilizes. Next update in 60 minutes.

h1. Status page template: resolved
*Title:* Resolved: Hosted API errors and latency
*Body:* This incident is resolved. We will publish a follow-up with additional details after internal review.

h1. Direct customer email template (enterprise)
*Subject:* Update: Redwood Hosted API regional incident
*Body:* Hello,

We are currently mitigating a regional incident impacting the Redwood Hosted API. To restore service, we are failing over traffic to a secondary region. During failover, you may observe intermittent errors or increased latency.

We will provide the next update by <TIME>. If you have urgent questions, reply to this email and our on-call team will respond.

Regards,
Redwood Inference Support

h1. Support handoff notes
- Comms lead should post the chosen template in the incident channel for IC approval before publishing.
- If specific customers are heavily impacted, Support should reference their observed error rates from dashboards (avoid internal metric names in external emails).

title: Postmortem: No free GPUs in us-east caused elevated 503s (2025-12-18)
preview:
h2. Summary
On 2025-12-18, us-east experienced a period with effectively zero free GPU slots across the primary hosted pool. Admission control rejected requests with 503 "no capacity" and queue wait times climbed for accepted requests.

h2. Customer impact
* Time window: 18:2219:05 ET
* Symptoms: increased 503s, long queue wait time, degraded p95 latency
* Affected: hosted API (us-east); spillover to eu-west increased p99 latency there

h2. Detection
* Alert: "gpu_pool_free_slots == 0"
* Status page internal note opened by oncall

h2. Root cause
A region-level routing rule intended for a planned maintenance window remained enabled after the window, preventing cross-region overflow early in the event.

h2. Resolution
* Disabled the stale routing rule
* Increased cross-region spillover budget for hosted tier for 24 hours
* Added temporary manual capacity for peak

h2. Action items
1) Add automated expiration for maintenance routing rules (Owner: SRE)
2) Add preflight checklist item in change-management for routing toggles (Owner: Eng Platform)

h2. References
* Related Jira: INT-1879
* Related Confluence: policies-and-process/change-management/maintenance-window-checklist

title: Post-incident summary template (publishable)
preview:
## Purpose
This template is used to produce **customer-publishable** post-incident summaries for Redwood’s public status page and linked surfaces (e.g., Console). The goal is to provide a timely, accurate account of:
- **Customer impact** (what customers experienced)
- **Timeline** (when it started, when it was mitigated, when it was fully resolved)
- **Root cause class** (what kind of failure occurred, without leaking sensitive details)
- **Mitigations and prevention** (what we did immediately and what we are changing)

This template is designed to be:
- Clear to a non-Redwood audience
- Consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private messaging
- Safe to publish (see confidentiality rules below)

---

## When to publish
Use this summary when an incident is customer-visible and any of the following are true:
- Severity is **Sev0–Sev2** (or equivalent) OR
- The incident resulted in a public status page incident OR
- Support/CS requests a publishable follow-up due to customer escalation

### Timing targets (guidance, not a commitment)
- **Sev0/Sev1:** publish within **2 business days** of resolution
- **Sev2:** publish within **5 business days** of resolution
- **Sev3 or minor:** publish only if there is meaningful customer learning or repeated pattern

If details are still under investigation, publish a summary with what we know and explicitly note what remains unknown.

---

## Audience and tone
**Audience:** external customers, prospects, and partners.

**Tone principles:**
- Plain language; avoid internal jargon
- Avoid speculation; be explicit about uncertainty
- Focus on impact, actions taken, and prevention
- Do not over-promise; avoid stating or implying new SLAs

---

## Approval and publication workflow
1) Draft is prepared by **Comms Lead** (or Incident Commander if no Comms Lead assigned).
2) Required reviewers:
   - **Incident Commander (IC)**
   - **SRE on-call / service owner** for technical accuracy
   - **Support/CS (on duty lead)** for customer clarity and escalation alignment
3) Conditional reviewers:
   - **Legal (Michael Grant)** if there is any contractual language risk, vendor attribution, or potential liability
   - **Security/Compliance (Naomi Feldman)** if there is any data access concern, suspicious activity, or regulatory relevance
4) Publish to the status page and link in Console when applicable.

Reference: incident comms roles page for exact responsibilities and escalation paths.

---

## Publishable post-incident summary (copy/paste template)

### Title
**{{Short customer-facing title}}**

Guidance:
- Include product surface + region when applicable (e.g., “Hosted API (us-east) elevated 5xx errors”)
- Avoid internal service names (e.g., “scheduler-v2”, “gateway-canary”)

**Incident ID:** {{INC-####}} (internal)  
**Status page incident:** {{link}} (if applicable)

---

### Summary
{{2–4 sentences describing what happened, where, and that service is now operating normally.}}

Recommended structure:
- What: {{error increase / elevated latency / streaming disconnects / partial outage}}
- Where: {{component(s), region(s)}}
- Customer effect: {{requests failing / timeouts / delayed responses}}
- Current state: {{mitigated at X, resolved at Y, monitoring completed}}

Example phrasing:
“Between {{start}} and {{end}}, some customers experienced {{impact}} affecting {{components}} in {{region}}. We mitigated the issue by {{high-level mitigation}} and service has been operating normally since {{resolved_time}}. We are implementing additional safeguards to prevent recurrence.”

---

### Customer impact
**Affected products/components:** {{Status page component list}}  
**Regions:** {{e.g., us-east only / multiple regions}}  
**Impact type:** {{Partial outage / Degraded performance}}  
**Customer symptoms:**
- {{Symptom 1 (customer observable)}}
- {{Symptom 2}}

**Who was impacted:** {{e.g., “a subset of requests”, “customers using Streaming”, “requests routed to us-east”}}  
**Error/latency characteristics (high-level):**
- {{e.g., “elevated 5xx error rates”, “p95 latency increased”}}

**Duration:** {{X minutes/hours}}  

Notes:
- Use ranges or qualitative descriptions where exact numbers are sensitive (see Confidentiality).
- Do not identify specific customers.

---

### What happened (high-level)
{{A short narrative that explains the system-level failure mode and why customers saw impact.}}

Include:
- Trigger (what changed or what event occurred)
- Failure mode (what broke and how it manifested)
- Why it propagated (why it affected customers vs being contained)

Avoid:
- Internal hostnames, cluster names, ticket links, or vendor escalation details
- “Blame-y” language

---

### Timeline (all times in UTC)
Provide a concise timeline of customer-relevant milestones.

| Time (UTC) | Event |
|---|---|
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Issue began (first confirmed customer impact) |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Detection (automated alert and/or customer report) |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Incident declared; investigation started |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Mitigation applied (high-level description) |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Impact reduced/contained |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Fully resolved |
| {{YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM}} | Monitoring complete |

Guidance:
- Keep events to 6–10 lines.
- If detection came from customers, say so (“We first learned via customer reports…”).

---

### Root cause classification
Select one primary class and (optional) a contributing factor class.

**Primary root cause class:** {{choose one}}
- Change-related (deployment/configuration)
- Capacity/overload (resource exhaustion, saturation)
- Dependency failure (cloud provider / upstream service)
- Networking/region routing
- Data plane bug (runtime/inference path)
- Control plane bug (scheduling/orchestration)
- Observability gap (detection/alerting deficiency)
- Human/process issue
- Security-related (only if approved by Security/Legal)
- Other (explain)

**Contributing factors (optional):** {{choose one or more}}
- Alerting thresholds too lax / missing signal
- Incorrect component mapping / status communication delay
- Rollback/mitigation tooling friction
- Insufficient canary coverage
- Runbook gaps

**Why this happened:** {{1–3 sentences; avoid internal-only detail.}}

---

### Mitigation and recovery
**Immediate mitigations:**
- {{Mitigation 1}} (e.g., traffic shift, rollback, feature flag, rate limiting, fallback)
- {{Mitigation 2}}

**Verification:**
- {{How we verified recovery}} (e.g., error rate returned to normal range, latency recovered, streaming disconnects normalized)

**Customer actions (if any):**
- {{Usually: “No customer action is required.”}}  
If required, keep it simple and safe (e.g., retry guidance) and coordinate with Support.

---

### Prevention and follow-ups
List concrete improvements with owners and target dates. Only include items we intend to do.

| Action | Type (Prevent/Detect/Respond) | Owner team | Target date |
|---|---|---|---|
| {{Action 1}} | {{Prevent/Detect/Respond}} | {{team}} | {{YYYY-MM-DD}} |
| {{Action 2}} | {{Prevent/Detect/Respond}} | {{team}} | {{YYYY-MM-DD}} |

Guidance:
- Prefer 3–6 high-confidence actions over a long list.
- If a long-term project is required, state the next concrete milestone.

---

### Additional notes
Optional, include:
- What went well (1–2 bullets)
- What we will improve in incident response/comms (1–2 bullets)
- Links to relevant product documentation (public) if helpful

---

## Confidentiality and redaction guidance (must follow)
This summary is intended to be **publishable**. Do not include the following unless explicitly approved by Legal and Security:

### Do not include
- Customer names, account identifiers, IPs, request IDs, or screenshots/log excerpts that could identify a customer
- Exact capacity numbers, utilization ceilings, GPU inventory, or region-by-region headroom
- Internal service names, cluster names, hostnames, repository paths, or private dashboard links
- Vendor escalation transcripts, provider ticket IDs, or attribution implying fault unless contractually cleared
- Security-sensitive details (attack indicators, suspected actor details, exploited paths)

### OK to include (recommended)
- Component-level impact (Hosted API / Streaming / Embeddings / Reranking / Console) and region(s)
- High-level metrics described qualitatively (“elevated error rates”, “increased latency”)
- High-level mitigations (“rolled back a configuration change”, “shifted traffic to another region”, “enabled fallback routing”)
- Root cause *class* and prevention steps

### Dedicated and Private incidents
- **Dedicated:** You may state that a Dedicated component in a region experienced impact if it was broadly applicable, but avoid implying customer-specific capacity constraints. If impact was limited to a single tenant, coordinate with Support/CS for the customer-specific follow-up and keep the public summary generalized or omit if not broadly relevant.
- **Private (VPC/on-prem):** Do not publish customer-specific Private outages publicly unless explicitly agreed with the customer and approved by Legal/Security. If a platform-wide issue affected Private deployments generally, publish at an appropriate abstraction level.

### If you are unsure
Escalate to:
- Support lead (for customer sensitivity)
- Legal (Michael Grant)
- Security/Compliance (Naomi Feldman)

---

## Quality checklist (before publishing)
- [ ] Impact describes **what customers experienced** (not internal-only symptoms)
- [ ] Components and regions match the status page component taxonomy
- [ ] Timeline is in **UTC** and includes start/mitigation/resolution
- [ ] Root cause is framed as a **class** with a clear explanation
- [ ] Follow-ups are specific and have owners + target dates
- [ ] No sensitive internal details, customer-identifying info, or over-promising language
- [ ] Support/CS has reviewed the wording

---

## Common wording blocks (optional)
Use these to stay consistent across summaries:

**No customer action required**
“No customer action is required. If you continue to see issues, please contact Support with timestamps and the affected region.”

**Uncertainty acknowledgement**
“We have identified the contributing factors and are continuing to investigate the underlying conditions that made this failure possible. We will update this summary if materially new information becomes available.”

**Status page + Console linking**
“Incident updates were posted to our status page during the event. This summary is provided as a follow-up with additional context and prevention steps.”

title: KMS Outage Game Day — Plan + Results (2025 Q4)
preview:
## Summary
This game day exercised Redwood’s KMS dependency under controlled failure conditions (hard outage + throttling + permission misconfig) to validate:
- KMS client wrapper behavior (timeouts, retries, jitter, error taxonomy)
- Circuit breaker + degrade-mode policy to prevent retry storms / cascading failures
- Observability (dashboards + alerts + traces) and on-call runbook readiness
- Customer-facing blast radius assumptions for Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments

We ran the scenarios in staging and a dedicated canary slice (internal traffic only). We did **not** run fault injection in production.

**High-level outcome:** Core availability targets were mostly met for new requests, but we observed elevated tail latency during partial KMS degradation and two operator-experience gaps (noisy alerts + unclear next steps for AccessDenied). Follow-up work was created and is tracked in the KMS hardening epic.

---

## Logistics
**Date/Time:** 2025-11-21 10:00–11:30 PT

**Facilitator:** Rafael Mendes (SRE)

**Primary participants:**
- Sean Gallagher (SRE) — on-call simulation lead
- Allison Grant (Observability) — dashboards/alert tuning
- Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane) — config/flags
- Benji Okafor (Platform) — wrapper integration support
- Marcus Lin (Security Eng) — policy expectations / risk tradeoffs
- Kevin Osei (Security Eng) — audit + security telemetry
- Logan Wright (Telemetry platform) — tracing conventions
- Tomáš Novák (SRE) — runbook validation + comms templates
- Fiona McCarthy (SecOps) — incident command / comms rehearsal

**Observers:** Priya Natarajan (Exec sponsor), Dr. Aisha Rahman (Exec sponsor)

**Comms channels:**
- Slack: #incidents (simulated), #eng-sre (coordination), #eng-security (policy clarifications)
- Incident template used: “SEV-2 / dependency degradation (KMS)”

**Environments / scope:**
- Staging (primary): `staging-us-east-1` and `staging-eu-west-1`
- Canary slice: internal traffic only, 1% of staging load generator
- Services exercised:
  - `control-plane-api` (key version metadata + rotation flags)
  - `data-plane-gateway` (request auth + envelope decrypt path)
  - `secrets-agent` (service-to-service secret material decrypt)

**Safety controls:**
- Feature flags defaulted to “safe”: circuit breaker enabled, retry budget guardrails enabled
- Load generation capped (token throughput limited) to avoid saturating shared staging infra
- Automatic rollback criteria in place (see “Stop Conditions”)

---

## Objectives and Success Criteria
### Primary objectives
1) Validate that KMS timeouts/throttling do not create a retry storm that amplifies KMS load.
2) Confirm circuit breaker behavior: open quickly under sustained failures and recover cleanly.
3) Verify expected end-user behavior: deterministic failures for decrypt paths, no silent data corruption, and clear operator logs.
4) Validate dashboards/alerts provide fast diagnosis (latency, error code, throttling, AccessDenied, InvalidCiphertext).
5) Ensure runbook enables mitigation within 10 minutes without deep KMS expertise.

### Success criteria (pass/fail)
- **No cascading failure:** `data-plane-gateway` 5xx rate increases < 2% sustained for internal canary traffic under throttling scenario; under full outage, failures should be limited to decrypt-dependent endpoints and be attributed correctly.
- **Circuit breaker:** opens within 30–60s of sustained KMS failure and reduces KMS request rate by > 80% compared to baseline retry behavior.
- **Latency cap:** p99 request latency should not exceed 2x baseline for non-decrypt-critical paths; decrypt-dependent paths may fail fast.
- **Observability:** dashboards show correct error taxonomy within 2 minutes; traces include KMS spans with consistent tags.
- **Operator usability:** on-call can identify root cause category (Timeout vs Throttling vs AccessDenied) and perform the correct first mitigation step in < 10 minutes.

**Stop conditions:**
- Any sign of data corruption / decrypt success with incorrect key material
- Any uncontrolled request storm (> 3x baseline KMS QPS for > 2 minutes)
- Any staging instability outside the KMS dependency surface (e.g., cluster-wide saturation)

---

## Pre-Game Day Prep
### Preconditions
- KMS client wrapper deployed to staging: timeouts + structured error mapping + basic metrics
- Circuit breaker feature flag available and enabled in staging
- Dashboard baseline established for 24h prior

### Tools used for fault injection
- Network fault injection via `chaosproxy` sidecar for outbound KMS calls (drop/timeout) in staging
- KMS throttling simulation via wrapper-level fault injector (rate-limited responses for `Decrypt` and `GenerateDataKey` calls)
- IAM AccessDenied simulation by swapping service role to a restricted policy for 10 minutes

### Key dashboards and queries
- Dashboard: “KMS Dependency Health (by service / region)”
  - `kms.client.latency_ms` (p50/p95/p99)
  - `kms.client.errors_total` tagged by `error_category` and `cloud_provider`
  - `kms.circuit_breaker.state` (closed/open/half-open)
  - `kms.throttle_budget.utilization`
- Log query (structured): `service:data-plane-gateway AND event:kms_call AND error_category:*`
- Trace search: `span.name:kms.decrypt` filtered by `error_category`

---

## Scenario Plan
### Scenario 1 — Full KMS outage (timeouts)
**What we did:**
- Injected 100% timeout on outbound KMS calls for `staging-us-east-1` for `data-plane-gateway` only.
- Duration: 12 minutes.

**Expected behavior:**
- Circuit breaker opens and stops hammering KMS.
- Decrypt-dependent operations fail fast with actionable error (`KMS_TIMEOUT`) and include remediation hint.
- Non-decrypt-critical routes remain within latency cap.
- Alerts:
  - “KMS error rate high” should fire (staging, non-paging)
  - “KMS circuit breaker open” should fire (warning)

### Scenario 2 — KMS throttling (429 / throttling)
**What we did:**
- Simulated KMS throttling starting at 20% then ramping to 60% for 15 minutes.
- Applied to both `Decrypt` and `GenerateDataKey` paths.

**Expected behavior:**
- Wrapper uses bounded retries with exponential backoff + jitter.
- Throttle budgets engage and backpressure reduces concurrency.
- Tail latency rises but stays below p99 cap for non-decrypt-critical routes.
- Alerts should distinguish throttling from timeout.

### Scenario 3 — Permission misconfiguration (AccessDenied)
**What we did:**
- Temporarily removed `kms:Decrypt` permission from the `secrets-agent` role in staging-eu-west-1.
- Duration: 8 minutes.

**Expected behavior:**
- Errors are categorized as `KMS_ACCESS_DENIED` with principal + key ARN in logs (redacted where needed).
- Runbook points to IAM remediation and verification steps.
- No retries for AccessDenied (fail fast).

### Scenario 4 — Key material mismatch / InvalidCiphertext (safety check)
**What we did:**
- Injected invalid ciphertext payloads for a small set of test secrets (non-production test fixtures).
- Duration: 5 minutes.

**Expected behavior:**
- Categorize as `KMS_INVALID_CIPHERTEXT`.
- No fallback/dual-read attempt unless rotation dual-read is explicitly enabled.
- Operator sees clear guidance: indicates key mismatch / wrong key ID / wrong ciphertext.

---

## Execution Timeline (high level)
- 10:00 PT — Kickoff, success criteria + stop conditions reviewed
- 10:10 PT — Scenario 1 (full outage) begins
- 10:22 PT — Scenario 1 ends; recovery observation
- 10:30 PT — Scenario 2 (throttling) begins
- 10:45 PT — Scenario 2 ends; alert review
- 10:52 PT — Scenario 3 (AccessDenied) begins
- 11:00 PT — Scenario 3 ends; runbook walkthrough
- 11:05 PT — Scenario 4 (InvalidCiphertext) begins
- 11:12 PT — Scenario 4 ends; debrief begins
- 11:30 PT — Wrap; action items assigned

---

## Results
### Scenario 1 — Full outage (timeouts)
**Observed behavior:**
- Circuit breaker opened in ~45s after the first sustained wave of timeouts.
- KMS outbound request rate dropped ~88% after breaker opened (good).
- Decrypt-dependent routes returned 5xx quickly, but error payloads were inconsistent across services.

**Impact (staging canary):**
- `data-plane-gateway` 5xx peaked at 1.7% (under the 2% sustained threshold) for internal canary traffic.
- Tail latency increased for decrypt-dependent routes (expected), but we saw minor p99 spillover to a non-decrypt route due to shared worker pool saturation.

**Observability:**
- Dashboards correctly reflected `Timeout` category.
- Trace spans existed but were missing `kms_key_id_hash` tag in some services (reduced triage speed).

**Pass/Fail:** Partial pass.
- Pass: breaker behavior, request storm avoided.
- Gap: inconsistent error payloads + minor latency spillover.

### Scenario 2 — Throttling (429)
**Observed behavior:**
- Wrapper retries behaved as expected (bounded), but we still saw elevated p99 latency in `staging-us-east-1` when throttling reached 60%.
- Alerting was **too noisy**: latency-based alert fired repeatedly and lacked clear grouping by region/service.

**Impact:**
- p99 increased ~2.4x baseline for a subset of decrypt paths (above target).
- No sustained 5xx increase beyond threshold.

**Observability:**
- Error taxonomy correctly identified `Throttling`.
- Throttle budget utilization was visible, but not prominently surfaced in the default dashboard view.

**Pass/Fail:** Partial pass.
- Pass: no uncontrolled amplification.
- Gap: tail latency cap exceeded for some decrypt paths; alert tuning needed.

### Scenario 3 — AccessDenied
**Observed behavior:**
- Fail-fast behavior worked (no retries).
- Logs were missing a remediation hint in one service (“which permission is missing?”).
- The runbook had the right category but did not include the quickest verification command for IAM role identity in Private-like installs.

**Pass/Fail:** Pass with doc gap.

### Scenario 4 — InvalidCiphertext
**Observed behavior:**
- Errors were categorized correctly.
- No silent fallback.
- Operator guidance was still too generic (“invalid ciphertext”) and didn’t point to the top three likely causes.

**Pass/Fail:** Pass with messaging improvement needed.

---

## Key Gaps and Learnings
1) **Error payload consistency:** Some components surfaced generic 500s without the standardized `error_category` mapping and remediation hints.
2) **Worker pool coupling:** Under heavy KMS degradation, decrypt paths can saturate shared worker pools and slightly impact unrelated routes.
3) **Alert noise:** Latency alert thresholds were too sensitive and did not segment by environment/region; multiple duplicate pages would occur if this were prod.
4) **Dashboards need “first 2 minutes” view:** Throttle budget and circuit breaker state are present but not highlighted; on-call needed extra clicks.
5) **Runbook needs more actionable IAM checks:** AccessDenied triage steps were directionally correct but missing specific verification commands and common pitfalls.

---

## Evidence / Artifacts

---

## Appendix A — Expected System Behavior (operator cheatsheet)
- **Timeouts / outage:** breaker opens; fail fast for decrypt-required routes; do not retry indefinitely; reduce concurrency.
- **Throttling:** bounded retries; backoff+jitter; throttle budget/backpressure; expect latency increase but not uncontrolled 5xx.
- **AccessDenied:** no retries; surface principal + missing permission; immediate IAM fix path.
- **InvalidCiphertext:** treat as key mismatch / wrong key / corrupted ciphertext; no silent fallback; check rotation flags only if explicitly enabled.

---

## Appendix B — Next Game Day Proposal (2026 Q1)
- Add rotation dual-read window scenario (controlled cutover + rollback) once ADR-008 implementation is fully deployed in staging.
- Include Private-like environment with customer-managed KMS/HSM integration to validate operator guide end-to-end.
text:
## Summary
This game day exercised Redwood’s KMS dependency under controlled failure conditions (hard outage + throttling + permission misconfig) to validate:
- KMS client wrapper behavior (timeouts, retries, jitter, error taxonomy)
- Circuit breaker + degrade-mode policy to prevent retry storms / cascading failures
- Observability (dashboards + alerts + traces) and on-call runbook readiness
- Customer-facing blast radius assumptions for Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments

We ran the scenarios in staging and a dedicated canary slice (internal traffic only). We did **not** run fault injection in production.

**High-level outcome:** Core availability targets were mostly met for new requests, but we observed elevated tail latency during partial KMS degradation and two operator-experience gaps (noisy alerts + unclear next steps for AccessDenied). Follow-up work was created and is tracked in the KMS hardening epic.

---

## Logistics
**Date/Time:** 2025-11-21 10:00–11:30 PT

**Facilitator:** Rafael Mendes (SRE)

**Primary participants:**
- Sean Gallagher (SRE) — on-call simulation lead
- Allison Grant (Observability) — dashboards/alert tuning
- Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane) — config/flags
- Benji Okafor (Platform) — wrapper integration support
- Marcus Lin (Security Eng) — policy expectations / risk tradeoffs
- Kevin Osei (Security Eng) — audit + security telemetry
- Logan Wright (Telemetry platform) — tracing conventions
- Tomáš Novák (SRE) — runbook validation + comms templates
- Fiona McCarthy (SecOps) — incident command / comms rehearsal

**Observers:** Priya Natarajan (Exec sponsor), Dr. Aisha Rahman (Exec sponsor)

**Comms channels:**
- Slack: #incidents (simulated), #eng-sre (coordination), #eng-security (policy clarifications)

**Environments / scope:**
- Staging (primary): `staging-us-east-1` and `staging-eu-west-1`
- Canary slice: internal traffic only, 1% of staging load generator
- Services exercised:
  - `control-plane-api` (key version metadata + rotation flags)
  - `data-plane-gateway` (request auth + envelope decrypt path)
  - `secrets-agent` (service-to-service secret material decrypt)

**Safety controls:**
- Feature flags defaulted to “safe”: circuit breaker enabled, retry budget guardrails enabled
- Load generation capped (token throughput limited) to avoid saturating shared staging infra
- Automatic rollback criteria in place (see “Stop Conditions”)

### Primary objectives

2) Confirm circuit breaker behavior: open quickly under sustained failures and recover cleanly.
3) Verify expected end-user behavior: deterministic failures for decrypt paths, no silent data corruption, and clear operator logs.
4) Validate dashboards/alerts provide fast diagnosis (latency, error code, throttling, AccessDenied, InvalidCiphertext).
5) Ensure runbook enables mitigation within 10 minutes without deep KMS expertise.

### Success criteria (pass/fail)

title: Runbook: Status Page Updates (Component-level)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to post and maintain **customer-facing** status page updates during incidents at Redwood Inference. It is optimized for:
- **Component-level accuracy** (product surface × region × capability)
- **Fast time-to-first-update (TTFU)** with consistent language
- **Verification and anti-flap behavior** (avoid noisy toggling)
- Clear coordination across **#incidents** and **#support**

This runbook applies to **Hosted API**, **Console**, and **Dedicated control-plane** customer-visible components. For **Private** deployments, see “Dedicated/Private guidance” below.

---

## Roles (who does what)
See: *Incident Comms Roles and Responsibilities* for full definitions.

- **Incident Commander (IC):** owns incident execution and severity, approves scope/impact statements.
- **Comms Lead:** owns status page updates and external-facing wording; ensures TTFU targets.
- **Scribe:** maintains internal timeline; ensures key events/metrics are captured for post-incident summary.

Rule of thumb:
- If you are the IC and no Comms Lead is assigned within 5 minutes, assign one in incident-bot or designate in #incidents.

---

## Where updates happen
- **Public status page:** Source of truth for customer-visible service health.
- **#incidents (Slack):** Real-time internal coordination and approvals.
- **#support (Slack):** Customer impact intake + outbound coordination; Support needs heads-up before/when a public update goes out.

---

## SLAs and targets (internal)
These targets are operational goals (not customer contractual promises).

- **TTFU target**
  - Sev0/Sev1: first public update within **10 minutes** of declaration
  - Sev2: within **20 minutes**
  - Sev3: status page update optional; use judgment (see “When not to update”)

- **Update cadence guidance**
  - During active mitigation: every **30–60 minutes** or when state materially changes
  - During monitoring: at least every **60–120 minutes** (or sooner if customer impact changes)

---

## Pre-flight checklist (do this before publishing)
1) Confirm an incident exists (declared) and severity is set.
2) Identify affected **components** using the taxonomy (avoid “API” catch-all).
3) Identify affected **regions** (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-central).
4) Confirm **failure mode** (errors vs latency vs streaming disconnects) using the customer-facing signals standard.
5) Check for **known workarounds** (fallback routing, model variant fallback, retry guidance).
6) For Sev0/Sev1: confirm approval requirement (see “Approval workflow”).

---

## Component selection rules (high-signal guidance)
Reference: *Status Page Component Taxonomy*.

### Choose the narrowest accurate component set
- Prefer **capability-specific** components:
  - “Hosted API – Chat/Generation” vs “Hosted API”
  - “Hosted API – Streaming” when disconnects / stream stalls are dominant
  - “Hosted API – Embeddings” when embedding endpoints are failing
  - “Hosted API – Reranking” when rerank endpoints are failing
- Split by **region** whenever impact is region-bound.

### Marking impact correctly
- If impact is limited (subset of requests, subset of models, or a single region), use **Degraded Performance** or **Partial Outage** rather than “Major Outage,” and specify scope in the message.
- If only a small number of customers are affected due to customer-specific configs/capacity, do **not** imply broad outage. Use Support-led comms and see “Customer-specific impact (avoid on status page)”.

---

## Verification: what signals qualify for customer-visible status
Reference: *Customer-Facing Status Signals Standard*.

Use dashboards that match the component and region you intend to update. Minimum verification for Hosted API components:
- **Errors:** elevated 5xx / gateway errors beyond threshold
- **Latency:** p95/p99 beyond threshold with sustained duration
- **Streaming-specific:** disconnect rate / stalled streams above threshold
- **SLO burn:** component-level burn alerts firing (if available)

### Anti-flap guidance
To avoid toggling statuses during noisy periods:
- Don’t change a component state based on a single short spike.
- Prefer sustained windows (e.g., 10–15 minutes) unless severity is clearly Sev0.
- When recovery begins, move to **Monitoring** messaging first; avoid declaring “Resolved” until the recovery is sustained (see “Resolution criteria”).

---

## Fast path: use incident-bot to draft and publish updates
If incident-bot is available for the incident:

1) In the incident channel, run the status draft command (see pinned incident-bot help).
2) Fill required fields:
   - Component(s)
   - Region(s)
   - Capability (chat/streaming/embeddings/reranking/console/dedicated control-plane)
   - Impact start time (best known)
   - Current state (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved)
3) Verify the draft message aligns with the approved template (see “Message templates”).
4) For Sev0/Sev1: request approval in-thread before publish (see “Approval workflow”).
5) Publish via incident-bot. Confirm the update appears correctly on the public status page.
6) Post a short note in **#support** with:
   - Incident link
   - Components impacted
   - Customer-safe summary and any workaround

If incident-bot fails, use the manual path below.

---

## Manual path: update Statuspage directly (fallback)
Use this only if automation is degraded/unavailable.

1) Log into Statuspage (SSO).
2) Create or open the incident.
3) Select impacted components and set the correct state:
   - **Degraded Performance** (latency elevated, intermittent failures)
   - **Partial Outage** (meaningful portion of requests failing)
   - **Major Outage** (broad inability to use the component)
4) Write/update the incident message using the correct phase template.
5) Publish the update.
6) Copy the public incident link into **#incidents** and **#support**.
7) Ensure the internal incident timeline captures:
   - Time of update
   - Components/regions marked
   - Reasoning + supporting dashboards

---

## Message templates (what to say)
Reference: *Incident Communication Templates*.

### Investigating (initial update)
Include:
- What is impacted (component + region)
- What users may see (errors/latency/streaming disconnects)
- What we’re doing (“investigating,” “engaged on-call,” etc.)
Avoid:
- Root cause speculation
- Mentioning specific customers
- Over-promising ETAs

Suggested structure:
- “We are investigating an issue impacting [component] in [region]. Users may see [symptom]. We will provide an update within [time window].”

### Identified
Include:
- High-level cause class (e.g., “network connectivity issue,” “capacity constraint,” “dependency degradation”) without sensitive details
- Scope and current behavior

### Mitigating
Include:
- What mitigation is in progress (e.g., “traffic shifted,” “rollback,” “fallback enabled”)
- What customers should expect (improving vs still impacted)

### Monitoring
Include:
- Signals improving and being monitored
- Remaining risk (e.g., “some elevated latency may persist”)

### Resolved
Include:
- Confirmation of recovery
- Impact window (start/end, best known)
- Link/commitment to post-incident summary (if policy requires)

---

## Approval workflow (Sev0/Sev1)
For Sev0/Sev1 incidents, before publishing a customer-facing update:
- **Required:** IC approval in #incidents thread (or explicit delegation to Comms Lead)
- **When Support approval is required:** if the update includes customer action guidance, broad statements that may trigger inbound tickets, or any unusual limitation wording

Practical process:
1) Post draft in #incidents with “Requesting approval to publish.”
2) Tag the IC and (if needed) **Dev Patel** (Support) or on-duty Support lead.
3) Publish after “Approved” acknowledgment.

Do not block TTFU waiting on perfect wording. Publish a safe Investigating update once scope is verified.

---

## Dedicated / Private guidance (customer-sensitive)
### Dedicated
- If impact is on the **Dedicated control plane** (provisioning, scaling actions, auth/management plane), use the appropriate “Dedicated – Control Plane” component and region if applicable.
- If impact is restricted to a single customer’s dedicated cluster, do **not** disclose. Coordinate via Support/CS and the account channel.

### Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Private customer runtime failures generally should **not** be reflected on the public status page unless the issue is due to a shared Redwood-managed control plane or a broadly impacting distribution artifact.
- When in doubt, escalate to IC + Security/Compliance reviewer.

---

## When NOT to update the public status page
- The issue is fully internal and has no customer-visible symptoms.
- The issue is limited to a single customer due to customer-specific configuration/capacity.
- The issue is a planned maintenance that has already been communicated via the maintenance process.

If customers are reporting impact but signals are unclear:
- Post an **Investigating** update only after component/region scope is verified to avoid inaccurate broad outage claims.
- Use #support to collect examples (request IDs, timestamps, region) and validate quickly.

---

## Coordination with #support (required steps)
When you publish or materially change an incident state:
1) Post in #support:
   - Public incident link
   - Components/regions affected
   - Short summary of symptoms
   - Workaround (only if confirmed safe)
   - Next expected update window
2) If Support reports conflicting customer experiences vs status page:
   - Ask for 2–3 concrete examples: request ID, region, endpoint, timestamp
   - Validate against component dashboards and adjust scope if needed

Reference: *Status Page Customer Escalation Playbook*.

---

## Resolution criteria (when to mark Resolved)
A component can be marked Resolved when:
- Errors and/or latency are back within thresholds for a sustained window (per standard)
- Mitigation is complete or rollback is stable
- There is no active burn alert attributable to the component
- Support has no ongoing wave of new tickets indicating continued broad impact

If there is uncertainty, remain in **Monitoring** and communicate what you’re watching.

---

## Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)
- **Pitfall:** Marking “Hosted API” when only embeddings is failing.
  - Fix: Use capability component; confirm via endpoint-tagged error panels.

- **Pitfall:** Marking all regions during a single-region event.
  - Fix: Validate region panels; only mark impacted regions.

- **Pitfall:** Overstating confidence/ETA early.
  - Fix: Use Investigating template language; commit to next update window, not resolution time.

- **Pitfall:** Toggling status repeatedly during mitigation.
  - Fix: Follow anti-flap guidance; prefer Monitoring state before Resolved.

---

## Post-incident requirements (handoff)
Before closing the incident:
- Ensure the incident timeline includes:
  - Impacted components/regions
  - Start time (best known) and end time
  - Mitigations executed and when
  - Links to key dashboards and any anomalies
- Confirm whether a **publishable post-incident summary** is required (policy-dependent). If required:
  - Assign an owner and due date
  - Link the status page incident to the summary record

Reference: *Post-Incident Summary Template*.

---

## Quick reference: minimal steps during a Sev1
1) Assign Comms Lead.
2) Verify component + region scope (dashboards).
3) Publish Investigating update (within 10 minutes).
4) Notify #support with link + summary.
5) Update state as knowledge changes (Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring).
6) Resolve only after sustained recovery; ensure timeline is complete.

---

## Change log
- 2025-12-03: Added explicit Dedicated/Private guidance; clarified Support approval triggers; aligned resolution criteria with anti-flap standard.
- 2025-11-20: Updated automation steps to match incident-bot statuspage integration; added #support coordination checklist.
- 2025-11-12: Initial publication for Status Page Transparency Upgrades rollout.

## Purpose
This runbook describes how to post and maintain **customer-facing** status page updates during incidents at Redwood Inference. It is optimized for:
- **Component-level accuracy** (product surface × region × capability)
- **Fast time-to-first-update (TTFU)** with consistent language
- **Verification and anti-flap behavior** (avoid noisy toggling)
- Clear coordination across **#incidents** and **#support**

This runbook applies to **Hosted API**, **Console**, and **Dedicated control-plane** customer-visible components. For **Private** deployments, see “Dedicated/Private guidance” below.

---

## Roles (who does what)
See: *Incident Comms Roles and Responsibilities* for full definitions.

- **Incident Commander (IC):** owns incident execution and severity, approves scope/impact statements.
- **Comms Lead:** owns status page updates and external-facing wording; ensures TTFU targets.
- **Scribe:** maintains internal timeline; ensures key events/metrics are captured for post-incident summary.

Rule of thumb:
- If you are the IC and no Comms Lead is assigned within 5 minutes, assign one in incident-bot or designate in #incidents.

---

## Where updates happen
- **Public status page:** Source of truth for customer-visible service health.
- **#incidents (Slack):** Real-time internal coordination and approvals.
- **#support (Slack):** Customer impact intake + outbound coordination; Support needs heads-up before/when a public update goes out.

---

## SLAs and targets (internal)
These targets are operational goals (not customer contractual promises).

- **TTFU target**
  - Sev0/Sev1: first public update within **10 minutes** of declaration

  - Sev3: status page update optional; use judgment (see “When not to update”)

---

## Fast path: use incident-bot to draft and publish updates

2) Fill required fields:
   - Component(s)

   - Current state (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved)
3) Verify the draft message aligns with the approved template (see “Message templates”).
4) For Sev0/Sev1: request approval in-thread before publish (see “Approval workflow”).
5) Publish via incident-bot. Confirm the update appears correctly on the public status page.
6) Post a short note in **#support** with:
   - Incident link
   - Components impacted
   - Customer-safe summary and any workaround

If incident-bot fails, use the manual path below.

---

   - **Major Outage** (broad inability to use the component)
4) Write/update the incident message using the correct phase template.
5) Publish the update.

---

title: operator-continuity-and-customer-status-playbook
preview:
Operator continuity and customer status playbook

Short description
This playbook standardizes shift continuity, evidence capture, escalation, and customer-facing status messaging for incidents that affect inference availability or quality across Redwood Inference hosted, dedicated, and private deployments. Use this as the single source of truth for handoffs, status automation, and post-incident evidence packaging.

Audience
- On-call engineers, SREs, incident commanders
- Customer Success and support engineers
- Applied ML and runtime engineers involved in mitigation or rollbacks

Scope
- Applies to incidents that impact: API availability, request latency, model correctness, routing/fallback behavior, or system capacity that materially affects customer SLAs.
- Excludes routine maintenance (see maintenance notification SOP).

Primary contacts and ownership
- Document owner_team: engineering
- On-call program owner: Priya Natarajan (SRE)
- Incident commander escalation pool: serving-runtime-eng, infra-sre
- Customer success escalation: Marco Chen (CS)
- Author: Daniela Ruiz

Incident classification quick reference
1. Severity: Major (P0)
   - Definition: Global outage, or >25% of requests failing across region(s), or customer-visible data loss.
   - Immediate actions: Pager bridge, incident command, customer impact classification.
2. Severity: High (P1)
   - Definition: Significant degradation (p50/p95 latency >2x baseline for >15m) or single-customer impacting errors >2%
   - Immediate actions: Triage, targeted mitigation, CS notification if revenue-impacting.
3. Severity: Medium (P2)
   - Definition: Localized or intermittent issues not causing immediate business interruption (e.g., model drift signals).
   - Actions: Investigate, evaluate need for rollback/canary.

Rotation, shifts, and handoffs
- Rotation cadence: 1-week primary (Mon 09:00 UTC -> Mon 09:00 UTC) + 24/7 secondary on 12-hour overlap for peak regions (APAC/EU/NA).
- Escalation window rules: 09:00-18:00 local business: page primary first; outside business hours: page primary then secondary after 5 minutes if unsnoozed.
- Shift handoff ledger (required in Slack #oncall-handoff and incident system):
  - Summary of active incidents (ID, severity, brief status)
  - Open tickets/owners with expected next action and ETA
  - Outstanding mitigations or runbook overrides
  - Scheduled maintenance or capacity sweeps during shift
  - Contact matrix for CS or legal notifications (if applicable)
- Minimum handoff message (one-line header + bulleted details) and tag the incoming on-call.

Handoff checklist (to perform within first 10 minutes)
1. Confirm bridge link and access works (copy one quick command to join).
2. Verify monitoring dashboards and alert groups are loading.
3. Confirm any temporary runbook patches are documented in the incident.
4. Acknowledge outstanding pages and set a 5-minute round-robin check-in cadence for active incidents.

Evidence capture (required fields for P0/P1 incidents)
- Use the incident evidence template below and attach artifacts to the incident ticket/artifact store. Fields are required strings or lists:
  - incident_id
  - first_detected (UTC iso format)
  - affected_services (comma-separated list)
  - affected_routes_or_models (comma-separated list)
  - observed_impact_summary (one-line)
  - monitoring_links (comma-separated URLs)
  - key_traces_or_logs (comma-separated URLs)
  - core_commands_run (preserve full command strings, timestamped)
  - mitigation_actions (who, what, when)
  - triage_owner (name + pager handle)

Evidence artifact example (plain text to attach)
```
incident_id: INF-2025-082
first_detected: 2025-11-05T18:12:00Z
affected_services: serving-api, routing-proxy
affected_routes_or_models: /v1/chat, model-redwood-1b-quant
observed_impact_summary: p95 latency increased from 420ms -> 2.1s; errors rose to 4.6%
monitoring_links: https://grafana.redwood/d/latency, https://sentry.redwood/issue/12345
key_traces_or_logs: https://jaeger.redwood/trace/abcd, s3://ops-artifacts/INF-2025-082/logs.tgz
core_commands_run: kubectl -n serving exec -it deploy/router -- curl -sS http://localhost/metrics > /tmp/router-metrics.txt
mitigation_actions: traffic-split to fallback (who: T. Morales, when: 2025-11-05T18:20Z)
triage_owner: Aisha Khan (@aisha)
```

Incident lifecycle and quick decision ladder
- Standard lifecycle: Detect -> Triage -> Notify/Bridge -> Mitigate -> Stabilize -> Communicate -> Postmortem.
- Quick decision ladder for routing/fallback in 3 steps:
  1. Can a compatible model variant be routed in <90s? If yes, perform controlled traffic-split (10/90 -> 50/50) and monitor SLOs for 10 minutes.
  2. If routing unavailable, enable cache-serving for idempotent read endpoints or engage edge-caching policy.
  3. If neither mitigates and customer impact is material, trigger Major outage declaration and publish public status; escalate CS to proactive outreach.

Runbook index (pointer names only)
- p0-major-outage-runbook
- p1-degradation-and-routing-fallback
- kv-cache-pressure-mitigation
- throttle-backpressure-sop
- model-quality-regression-escalation
- status-publish-and-visibility-sop
(See runbook library for step-by-step commands and play artifacts.)

Operator continuity signals (novel section)
- Continuity Signal Matrix (operators mark these at handoff):
  - GREEN: No active incidents; all alerts nominal; no outstanding mitigations.
  - AMBER: Minor incidents or ongoing mitigations; recommended 30m checkpoint.
  - RED: Active P1/P0 incident or unresolved escalations; require immediate overlap and takeover briefing.
- Incoming on-call must reply to handoff message with their signal state within 5 minutes.

Customer status taxonomy and multi-channel templates
- Status levels: OPERATIONAL, PARTIAL_DEGRADATION, MAJOR_OUTAGE, SECURITY_INCIDENT (restricted).
- Publish rules: P0 -> MAJOR_OUTAGE public; P1 -> PARTIAL_DEGRADATION (public) unless customer-sensitive data exposed.

Web status page templates (short concise language)
- Initial acknowledgement (web):
Title: We are investigating increased errors and latency
Body: We are aware of increased errors and elevated latency for inference requests beginning at {first_detected}. Our engineers are actively investigating and will provide an update within 15 minutes. Incident ID: {incident_id}

- Mitigation update (web):
Title: Mitigation in progress
Body: We have applied a routing fallback to an alternate model and adjusted traffic allocations to reduce errors. We are monitoring consumer impact and will update shortly. Affected regions: {regions}

- Resolution (web):
Title: Service restored
Body: Service has been restored and metrics have returned to baseline. We reverted temporary routing changes and will publish a postmortem within the timeline below. If you continue to experience issues contact support.

Slack channel updates (internal)
- Post a one-line status and link to incident ticket, then paste the public status body. Example:
[INF-2025-082] Investigating elevated latency -> bridge: {link} -> status: PARTIAL_DEGRADATION

Email/SMS template (CS friendly)
Subject: Redwood Inference — Service impact {incident_id}
Body: Hello {Customer},
We are investigating an issue affecting inference latency since {first_detected}. Our team has applied temporary mitigations. We will follow up within {cadence}. If this impacts critical paths please reply or contact your CSM.

Automated publish tooling (example)
- CLI: status-publish --id {incident_id} --level PARTIAL_DEGRADATION --title "Investigating" --body "..."
- API (curl):
```
curl -X POST https://status-api.internal/incidents -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_OPS_TOKEN" -d '{"id":"{incident_id}","state":"PARTIAL_DEGRADATION","title":"Investigating","body":"..."}'
```

Customer communication cadence and SLAs
- P0: initial public notice <= 10 minutes; updates every 30 minutes; final resolution + postmortem draft <= 48 hours.
- P1: initial notice <= 30 minutes; hourly updates while active; postmortem draft <= 7 days if customers materially affected.

Post-incident packaging requirements
- Assign postmortem owner within 12 hours for P0, 72 hours for P1.
- Required artifacts in final package: evidence template, minute-level timeline for first 2 hours, SLO dashboards (png links), configuration snapshots, and remediation steps with owners and target dates.

Postmortem minimal headings (to publish)
- Executive summary (1-3 lines)
- Impact (customers, regions, endpoints)
- Timeline (minute resolution for first 120 minutes)
- Root cause analysis
- Remediation and prevention (with owners & due dates)
- Communication timeline (what was sent to customers and when)

Game day framework (quarterly, focused on continuity)
- Purpose: validate handoff practices, evidence completeness, status publishing automation, and CS engagement.
- Typical participants: SRE, Serving Runtime, Applied ML, CS, Product.
- Timebox: 3-4 hours.
- Success criteria (operationalized):
  - Status page published within 10 minutes of incident creation.
  - Evidence template completed and attached within 60 minutes.
  - CS notified within 15 minutes for impacted customers.
- After-action: update any runbook gaps within 2 weeks; re-run scenario if continuity signal failed.

Operational KPIs (tracked by Ops Metrics)
- MTTA target: <= 2 minutes for P0, <= 10 minutes for P1
- MTMA (time to first mitigation): <= 15 minutes for P0
- Status publish SLA: <= 10 minutes for P0, <= 30 minutes for P1
- Evidence completeness: >= 95% for P0/P1 postmortems

Runbook governance
- Owners review and confirm runbooks at quarter start.
- Decommission runbooks not exercised in 18 months following a review and archived with rationale.
- Game day failures escalate to runbook rewrite and re-test within two weeks.

Appendix: quick commands and artifacts to preserve
- Join bridge: slack call {bridge_link} or run: bridge-join --id {incident_id}
- Collect logs example: kubectl -n serving logs deploy/proxy --since=30m > /tmp/proxy-logs.txt
- Snapshot SLO: curl -sS https://grafana.redwood/render?panelId=12&from=now-30m -o ./slo-panel.png

Related pages
- /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-comm-and-postmortem-framework-2026
- /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026

text:
Operator continuity and customer status playbook

Short description
This playbook standardizes shift continuity, evidence capture, escalation, and customer-facing status messaging for incidents that affect inference availability or quality across Redwood Inference hosted, dedicated, and private deployments. Use this as the single source of truth for handoffs, status automation, and post-incident evidence packaging.

Audience
- On-call engineers, SREs, incident commanders
- Customer Success and support engineers
- Applied ML and runtime engineers involved in mitigation or rollbacks

Scope
- Applies to incidents that impact: API availability, request latency, model correctness, routing/fallback behavior, or system capacity that materially affects customer SLAs.
- Excludes routine maintenance (see maintenance notification SOP).

Primary contacts and ownership
- Document owner_team: engineering
- On-call program owner: Priya Natarajan (SRE)
- Incident commander escalation pool: serving-runtime-eng, infra-sre
- Customer success escalation: Marco Chen (CS)
- Author: Daniela Ruiz

Incident classification quick reference
1. Severity: Major (P0)
   - Definition: Global outage, or >25% of requests failing across region(s), or customer-visible data loss.
   - Immediate actions: Pager bridge, incident command, customer impact classification.
2. Severity: High (P1)
   - Definition: Significant degradation (p50/p95 latency >2x baseline for >15m) or single-customer impacting errors >2%
   - Immediate actions: Triage, targeted mitigation, CS notification if revenue-impacting.
3. Severity: Medium (P2)
   - Definition: Localized or intermittent issues not causing immediate business interruption (e.g., model drift signals).
   - Actions: Investigate, evaluate need for rollback/canary.

Rotation, shifts, and handoffs
- Rotation cadence: 1-week primary (Mon 09:00 UTC -> Mon 09:00 UTC) + 24/7 secondary on 12-hour overlap for peak regions (APAC/EU/NA).
- Escalation window rules: 09:00-18:00 local business: page primary first; outside business hours: page primary then secondary after 5 minutes if unsnoozed.
- Shift handoff ledger (required in Slack #oncall-handoff and incident system):
  - Summary of active incidents (ID, severity, brief status)
  - Open tickets/owners with expected next action and ETA
  - Outstanding mitigations or runbook overrides
  - Scheduled maintenance or capacity sweeps during shift
  - Contact matrix for CS or legal notifications (if applicable)
- Minimum handoff message (one-line header + bulleted details) and tag the incoming on-call.

Handoff checklist (to perform within first 10 minutes)
1. Confirm bridge link and access works (copy one quick command to join).
2. Verify monitoring dashboards and alert groups are loading.
3. Confirm any temporary runbook patches are documented in the incident.
4. Acknowledge outstanding pages and set a 5-minute round-robin check-in cadence for active incidents.

Evidence capture (required fields for P0/P1 incidents)
- Use the incident evidence template below and attach artifacts to the incident ticket/artifact store. Fields are required strings or lists:
  - incident_id
  - first_detected (UTC iso format)
  - affected_services (comma-separated list)
  - affected_routes_or_models (comma-separated list)
  - observed_impact_summary (one-line)
  - monitoring_links (comma-separated URLs)
  - key_traces_or_logs (comma-separated URLs)
  - core_commands_run (preserve full command strings, timestamped)
  - mitigation_actions (who, what, when)
  - triage_owner (name + pager handle)

Evidence artifact example (plain text to attach)
```
incident_id: INF-2025-082
first_detected: 2025-11-05T18:12:00Z
affected_services: serving-api, routing-proxy
affected_routes_or_models: /v1/chat, model-redwood-1b-quant
observed_impact_summary: p95 latency increased from 420ms -> 2.1s; errors rose to 4.6%
monitoring_links: https://grafana.redwood/d/latency, https://sentry.redwood/issue/12345
key_traces_or_logs: https://jaeger.redwood/trace/abcd, s3://ops-artifacts/INF-2025-082/logs.tgz
core_commands_run: kubectl -n serving exec -it deploy/router -- curl -sS http://localhost/metrics > /tmp/router-metrics.txt
mitigation_actions: traffic-split to fallback (who: T. Morales, when: 2025-11-05T18:20Z)
triage_owner: Aisha Khan (@aisha)
```

Incident lifecycle and quick decision ladder
- Standard lifecycle: Detect -> Triage -> Notify/Bridge -> Mitigate -> Stabilize -> Communicate -> Postmortem.
- Quick decision ladder for routing/fallback in 3 steps:
  1. Can a compatible model variant be routed in <90s? If yes, perform controlled traffic-split (10/90 -> 50/50) and monitor SLOs for 10 minutes.
  2. If routing unavailable, enable cache-serving for idempotent read endpoints or engage edge-caching policy.
  3. If neither mitigates and customer impact is material, trigger Major outage declaration and publish public status; escalate CS to proactive outreach.

Runbook index (pointer names only)
- p0-major-outage-runbook
- p1-degradation-and-routing-fallback
- kv-cache-pressure-mitigation
- throttle-backpressure-sop
- model-quality-regression-escalation
- status-publish-and-visibility-sop
(See runbook library for step-by-step commands and play artifacts.)

Operator continuity signals (novel section)
- Continuity Signal Matrix (operators mark these at handoff):
  - GREEN: No active incidents; all alerts nominal; no outstanding mitigations.
  - AMBER: Minor incidents or ongoing mitigations; recommended 30m checkpoint.
  - RED: Active P1/P0 incident or unresolved escalations; require immediate overlap and takeover briefing.
- Incoming on-call must reply to handoff message with their signal state within 5 minutes.

Customer status taxonomy and multi-channel templates
- Status levels: OPERATIONAL, PARTIAL_DEGRADATION, MAJOR_OUTAGE, SECURITY_INCIDENT (restricted).
- Publish rules: P0 -> MAJOR_OUTAGE public; P1 -> PARTIAL_DEGRADATION (public) unless customer-sensitive data exposed.

Web status page templates (short concise language)
- Initial acknowledgement (web):
Title: We are investigating increased errors and latency
Body: We are aware of increased errors and elevated latency for inference requests beginning at {first_detected}. Our engineers are actively investigating and will provide an update within 15 minutes. Incident ID: {incident_id}

- Mitigation update (web):
Title: Mitigation in progress
Body: We have applied a routing fallback to an alternate model and adjusted traffic allocations to reduce errors. We are monitoring consumer impact and will update shortly. Affected regions: {regions}

- Resolution (web):
Title: Service restored
Body: Service has been restored and metrics have returned to baseline. We reverted temporary routing changes and will publish a postmortem within the timeline below. If you continue to experience issues contact support.

Slack channel updates (internal)
- Post a one-line status and link to incident ticket, then paste the public status body. Example:
[INF-2025-082] Investigating elevated latency -> bridge: {link} -> status: PARTIAL_DEGRADATION

Email/SMS template (CS friendly)
Subject: Redwood Inference — Service impact {incident_id}
Body: Hello {Customer},
We are investigating an issue affecting inference latency since {first_detected}. Our team has applied temporary mitigations. We will follow up within {cadence}. If this impacts critical paths please reply or contact your CSM.

Automated publish tooling (example)
- CLI: status-publish --id {incident_id} --level PARTIAL_DEGRADATION --title "Investigating" --body "..."
- API (curl):
```
curl -X POST https://status-api.internal/incidents -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_OPS_TOKEN" -d '{"id":"{incident_id}","state":"PARTIAL_DEGRADATION","title":"Investigating","body":"..."}'
```

Customer communication cadence and SLAs
- P0: initial public notice <= 10 minutes; updates every 30 minutes; final resolution + postmortem draft <= 48 hours.
- P1: initial notice <= 30 minutes; hourly updates while active; postmortem draft <= 7 days if customers materially affected.

Post-incident packaging requirements
- Assign postmortem owner within 12 hours for P0, 72 hours for P1.
- Required artifacts in final package: evidence template, minute-level timeline for first 2 hours, SLO dashboards (png links), configuration snapshots, and remediation steps with owners and target dates.

Postmortem minimal headings (to publish)
- Executive summary (1-3 lines)
- Impact (customers, regions, endpoints)
- Timeline (minute resolution for first 120 minutes)
- Root cause analysis
- Remediation and prevention (with owners & due dates)
- Communication timeline (what was sent to customers and when)

Game day framework (quarterly, focused on continuity)
- Purpose: validate handoff practices, evidence completeness, status publishing automation, and CS engagement.
- Typical participants: SRE, Serving Runtime, Applied ML, CS, Product.
- Timebox: 3-4 hours.
- Success criteria (operationalized):
  - Status page published within 10 minutes of incident creation.
  - Evidence template completed and attached within 60 minutes.
  - CS notified within 15 minutes for impacted customers.
- After-action: update any runbook gaps within 2 weeks; re-run scenario if continuity signal failed.

Operational KPIs (tracked by Ops Metrics)
- MTTA target: <= 2 minutes for P0, <= 10 minutes for P1
- MTMA (time to first mitigation): <= 15 minutes for P0
- Status publish SLA: <= 10 minutes for P0, <= 30 minutes for P1
- Evidence completeness: >= 95% for P0/P1 postmortems

Runbook governance
- Owners review and confirm runbooks at quarter start.
- Decommission runbooks not exercised in 18 months following a review and archived with rationale.
- Game day failures escalate to runbook rewrite and re-test within two weeks.

Appendix: quick commands and artifacts to preserve
- Join bridge: slack call {bridge_link} or run: bridge-join --id {incident_id}
- Collect logs example: kubectl -n serving logs deploy/proxy --since=30m > /tmp/proxy-logs.txt
- Snapshot SLO: curl -sS https://grafana.redwood/render?panelId=12&from=now-30m -o ./slo-panel.png

Related pages
- /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-comm-and-postmortem-framework-2026
- /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026

title: Incident Comms Roles & Responsibilities (Status Page + Customer Updates)
preview:
h1. Summary
This page defines the *roles, responsibilities, and approval rules* for customer-facing communications during incidents at Redwood Inference. It standardizes:
* Who owns public updates (Statuspage + customer-facing statements)
* When updates are required and how often they should happen
* How to coordinate with Support/CS so customer responses are consistent
* What content is allowed vs. prohibited (security/legal/customer sensitivity)

This process supports the “Status page transparency upgrades” initiative and is designed to reduce on-call load through templates + automation while improving time-to-first-update (TTFU) and accuracy.

h1. Scope
h2. In scope
* Public Statuspage updates for Hosted API, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private signals
* Coordination with Support/CS for ticket macros, escalations, and customer outreach
* Approval rules for high-severity incidents
* Publishable post-incident summaries (high-level, customer-safe)

h2. Out of scope
* Internal-only incident comms (engineering details) beyond required handoffs
* Customer-specific incident notifications (handled via Support/CS + account teams)
* Legal incident response (breach notification); follow Security/Compliance procedures

h1. Roles
h2. Required roles for Sev0/Sev1
* *Incident Commander (IC):* Owns incident response and technical decision-making.
* *Comms Lead (CL):* Owns all customer-facing communication during the incident.
* *Scribe:* Owns timeline capture and decision logging (used for post-incident summary).

For Sev2/Sev3, IC may combine roles if needed, but *Comms Lead duties must still be explicitly covered*.

h2. Role definitions (what “done” means)

h3. Incident Commander (IC)
Responsibilities:
* Declare severity and ensure roles are staffed (IC/CL/Scribe).
* Provide Comms Lead with accurate, current incident state:
  * impacted components/regions/capabilities
  * symptoms customers see
  * mitigation actions in progress
  * known/unknown status
* Decide on mitigation strategy and customer-impact tradeoffs.
* Ensure post-incident follow-ups are created and owned.

IC is *Accountable* for correctness of incident status, but *Comms Lead is Responsible* for publishing.

h3. Comms Lead (CL)
Responsibilities:
* Publish updates on the public Statuspage using approved templates.
* Maintain consistency across:
  * Statuspage incident updates
  * Support macros / ticket responses
  * Console linking / incident summary text (where enabled)
* Meet communication targets:
  * *TTFU:* first public update within target window (see “When to update”).
  * Update frequency appropriate to severity.
* Avoid prohibited content (see “Confidentiality + content rules”).
* Coordinate approvals (Sev0/Sev1) before publishing where required.
* Ensure a publishable post-incident summary is drafted promptly after resolution.

h3. Scribe
Responsibilities:
* Maintain an accurate incident timeline:
  * state changes (Investigating → Identified → Mitigating → Monitoring → Resolved)
  * mitigations applied
  * customer impact start/stop timestamps (best estimate)
  * links to dashboards, relevant PRs/rollbacks, and decisions
* Capture “customer-visible impact” phrasing as it evolves (for CL to reuse).

h3. Support/CS Lead (on-duty)
Responsibilities:
* Provide customer signal intake to the incident channel:
  * ticket volume spikes
  * enterprise escalations
  * customer-provided logs and timestamps
* Confirm if customer comms need to include guidance (workarounds, retries, fallback behavior).
* For Sev0/Sev1, act as an approval checkpoint when required (see “Approval rules”).

h1. RACI (quick reference)
|| Activity || IC || Comms Lead || Scribe || Support/CS Lead ||
| Declare severity | A/R | C | C | C |
| Assign roles | A/R | C | C | C |
| Decide impacted components | A/R | C | C | C |
| Draft Statuspage update | C | R | C | C |
| Publish Statuspage update | C | A/R | C | C |
| Support macros / ticket response alignment | C | R | C | A/R |
| Approval for Sev0/Sev1 public update | A | R | C | A (when required) |
| Post-incident summary draft | C | R | R | C |

Legend: R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted

h1. When to update the Statuspage
h2. Trigger conditions
Create or update a Statuspage incident when *any* of the below are true for a public component (see component taxonomy):
* Customer-impacting errors above threshold (sustained; anti-flap rules apply)
* Sustained latency degradation that meaningfully impacts customer workflows
* Widespread streaming disconnects/timeouts
* Major control-plane degradation that blocks usage (e.g., auth/session/route failures)
* A confirmed region-specific outage/degradation affecting a customer-visible capability

Do *not* wait for root cause confirmation to post the first update.

h2. Time-to-first-update (TTFU) targets
* *Sev0:* publish first public update within *15 minutes* of incident declaration.
* *Sev1:* publish first public update within *30 minutes*.
* *Sev2:* publish within *60 minutes* if customer impact is confirmed.
* *Sev3:* optional; use judgment (prefer Support macros only if minimal scope).

If you cannot meet TTFU due to ambiguity, publish a constrained Investigating update with known impact and what we’re checking.

h2. Update frequency guidance
* *Sev0:* every *15–30 minutes* until stable mitigation is in place.
* *Sev1:* every *30–60 minutes*.
* *Sev2:* at major state changes, at least every *2 hours* while ongoing.

Always update when transitioning states (Investigating → Identified → Mitigating → Monitoring → Resolved).

h2. Component selection rules (accuracy > breadth)
* Mark *only* the impacted product components and regions when evidence supports it.
* Prefer “partial degradation” wording when:
  * impact is limited to a subset of routes/models
  * impact is limited to a subset of customers
  * errors occur only under specific request patterns (e.g., long contexts)
* If uncertain, choose the *narrowest truthful scope* and explicitly note investigation.

For details: see *Status Page Component Taxonomy* and the *Customer-facing status signals standard*.

h1. Coordination model (Slack + Support/CS)
h2. Required channels
* Incident channel: canonical incident coordination.
* #incidents: announcements/major updates.
* #support (or designated Support incident channel): customer ticket coordination.

h2. Standard coordination loop
1) IC declares incident and assigns CL + Scribe.
2) CL posts in incident channel:
   * proposed impacted components/regions
   * proposed first Statuspage update time
   * request for Support/CS on-duty point of contact
3) Support/CS shares:
   * top customer-reported symptoms
   * any high-priority escalations requiring tailored responses
4) CL publishes Statuspage update and posts the exact text back into incident channel.
5) Support updates macros / ticket responses to match the same language (no contradictions).

h2. Customer escalations that dispute status accuracy
If a customer disputes the Statuspage accuracy:
* Support follows the *Status page customer escalation playbook*.
* CL and IC provide evidence links (dashboards) and decide whether to adjust components.
* If there’s any mismatch due to mapping bugs, create an action item tagged “status accuracy audit”.

h1. Approval rules
h2. What requires approval
Approval requirements apply to *public* updates (Statuspage) in these cases:
* *Sev0:* approval required prior to publishing the initial update and any update that changes scope (new regions/components) or declares “Resolved”.
* *Sev1:* approval required prior to publishing “Resolved” and any update that includes customer guidance/workarounds beyond retries.

h2. Who can approve
Any one of the following (in priority order):
* IC (always)
* Support/CS on-duty lead (recommended for Sev0/Sev1 customer-impact language)
* SRE leadership delegate (if IC is overloaded)

If IC is unavailable, Comms Lead may publish a minimal Investigating update to meet TTFU, then seek approval for subsequent updates.

h2. Lightweight approval process (keep it fast)
* Comms Lead posts the draft update text in the incident channel with “APPROVAL NEEDED” and tags approver.
* Approver replies “Approved” or provides edits.
* Comms Lead publishes and pastes the final text + Statuspage link back to the channel.

h1. Confidentiality + content rules (customer-safe language)
h2. Allowed content
* Symptoms customers may observe (errors, timeouts, disconnects)
* Affected surface area (component + region + capability)
* What we are doing (investigating, mitigating, monitoring)
* General mitigation categories (rerouting, capacity adjustments, rollback)
* High-level outcome and prevention steps (post-incident summary)

h2. Prohibited / restricted content
Do not publish:
* Customer-identifying details (names, IDs, traffic levels, “largest customer”, etc.)
* Vendor tickets, underlying cloud account details, IPs, hostnames, internal service names that expose architecture unexpectedly
* Precise capacity shortfalls tied to a single customer’s load or reservation
* Security-sensitive details (credentials, attack speculation)

If you are unsure, keep the update high-level and request Security/Legal review.

h2. Dedicated + Private messaging constraints
* Dedicated incidents: publish component-level impact only when it reflects a *general service capability* (e.g., Dedicated control plane) rather than a single customer’s isolated cluster.
* Private (VPC/on-prem): generally do not publish customer-specific outages on the public Statuspage. Use Support/CS channels and account-specific communication.

h1. Templates and tooling
h2. Templates
Use the standardized blocks in *Incident Communication Templates*.
* Investigating: confirm symptoms + scope + we’re investigating
* Identified: what we know (high-level) + next action
* Mitigating: mitigation in progress + expected next update window
* Monitoring: improvements observed + what we’re watching
* Resolved: impact window + confirmation of stability + link to summary (when available)

h2. Incident-bot automation expectations
* CL should use incident-bot to generate drafts when available; edit for accuracy and sensitivity.
* All public posts must be logged back to the incident channel for auditability.
* Use idempotent publishing (avoid duplicate updates) and ensure the incident timeline includes state changes.

h1. Operational checklist (Comms Lead)
1) Confirm you are assigned as Comms Lead.
2) Validate impacted components/regions using the component health dashboards.
3) Publish first Statuspage update within TTFU target (minimal + accurate).
4) Post the exact published text into the incident channel.
5) Coordinate with Support/CS on messaging alignment.
6) On major changes (scope, mitigation, recovery), publish an update.
7) Prior to “Resolved”, confirm:
   * metrics are stable (anti-flap guidance)
   * IC agrees incident is mitigated
   * approval obtained if Sev0/Sev1
8) Ensure the post-incident summary draft is created within the policy window.

h1. Exceptions and escalation
If you cannot staff Comms Lead due to on-call constraints:
* IC escalates to SRE on-call manager or SRE leadership delegate to assign a CL.
* If no CL is available, IC must either:
  * publish the minimal update themselves, or
  * explicitly delegate public comms to Support/CS lead (temporary) and document it in the channel.

h1. Change log
* 2025-11-26: Clarified approval checkpoints for Sev0/Sev1 and Dedicated/Private public visibility guidance.
* 2025-11-18: Added TTFU targets and update frequency guidelines.
* 2025-11-12: Initial publication (Comms Lead role + coordination loop).
