h2. Update frequency guidance
* *Sev0:* every *15–30 minutes* until stable mitigation is in place.
* *Sev1:* every *30–60 minutes*.
* *Sev2:* at major state changes, at least every *2 hours* while ongoing.

Always update when transitioning states (Investigating → Identified → Mitigating → Monitoring → Resolved).

h2. Component selection rules (accuracy > breadth)
* Mark *only* the impacted product components and regions when evidence supports it.
* Prefer “partial degradation” wording when:
  * impact is limited to a subset of routes/models
  * impact is limited to a subset of customers
  * errors occur only under specific request patterns (e.g., long contexts)
* If uncertain, choose the *narrowest truthful scope* and explicitly note investigation.

For details: see *Status Page Component Taxonomy* and the *Customer-facing status signals standard*.

h1. Coordination model (Slack + Support/CS)
h2. Required channels
* Incident channel: canonical incident coordination.
* #incidents: announcements/major updates.
* #support (or designated Support incident channel): customer ticket coordination.

h2. Standard coordination loop
1) IC declares incident and assigns CL + Scribe.
2) CL posts in incident channel:
   * proposed impacted components/regions
   * proposed first Statuspage update time
   * request for Support/CS on-duty point of contact
3) Support/CS shares:
   * top customer-reported symptoms
   * any high-priority escalations requiring tailored responses
4) CL publishes Statuspage update and posts the exact text back into incident channel.
5) Support updates macros / ticket responses to match the same language (no contradictions).

h2. Customer escalations that dispute status accuracy
If a customer disputes the Statuspage accuracy:
* Support follows the *Status page customer escalation playbook*.
* CL and IC provide evidence links (dashboards) and decide whether to adjust components.
* If there’s any mismatch due to mapping bugs, create an action item tagged “status accuracy audit”.

h1. Approval rules
h2. What requires approval
Approval requirements apply to *public* updates (Statuspage) in these cases:
* *Sev0:* approval required prior to publishing the initial update and any update that changes scope (new regions/components) or declares “Resolved”.
* *Sev1:* approval required prior to publishing “Resolved” and any update that includes customer guidance/workarounds beyond retries.

h2. Who can approve
Any one of the following (in priority order):
* IC (always)
* Support/CS on-duty lead (recommended for Sev0/Sev1 customer-impact language)
* SRE leadership delegate (if IC is overloaded)

If IC is unavailable, Comms Lead may publish a minimal Investigating update to meet TTFU, then seek approval for subsequent updates.

h2. Lightweight approval process (keep it fast)
* Comms Lead posts the draft update text in the incident channel with “APPROVAL NEEDED” and tags approver.
* Approver replies “Approved” or provides edits.
* Comms Lead publishes and pastes the final text + Statuspage link back to the channel.

h1. Confidentiality + content rules (customer-safe language)
h2. Allowed content
* Symptoms customers may observe (errors, timeouts, disconnects)
* Affected surface area (component + region + capability)
* What we are doing (investigating, mitigating, monitoring)
* General mitigation categories (rerouting, capacity adjustments, rollback)
* High-level outcome and prevention steps (post-incident summary)

h2. Prohibited / restricted content
Do not publish:
* Customer-identifying details (names, IDs, traffic levels, “largest customer”, etc.)
* Vendor tickets, underlying cloud account details, IPs, hostnames, internal service names that expose architecture unexpectedly
* Precise capacity shortfalls tied to a single customer’s load or reservation
* Security-sensitive details (credentials, attack speculation)

If you are unsure, keep the update high-level and request Security/Legal review.

h2. Dedicated + Private messaging constraints
* Dedicated incidents: publish component-level impact only when it reflects a *general service capability* (e.g., Dedicated control plane) rather than a single customer’s isolated cluster.
* Private (VPC/on-prem): generally do not publish customer-specific outages on the public Statuspage. Use Support/CS channels and account-specific communication.

h1. Templates and tooling
h2. Templates
Use the standardized blocks in *Incident Communication Templates*.
* Investigating: confirm symptoms + scope + we’re investigating
* Identified: what we know (high-level) + next action
* Mitigating: mitigation in progress + expected next update window
* Monitoring: improvements observed + what we’re watching
* Resolved: impact window + confirmation of stability + link to summary (when available)

h2. Incident-bot automation expectations
* CL should use incident-bot to generate drafts when available; edit for accuracy and sensitivity.
* All public posts must be logged back to the incident channel for auditability.
* Use idempotent publishing (avoid duplicate updates) and ensure the incident timeline includes state changes.

h1. Operational checklist (Comms Lead)
1) Confirm you are assigned as Comms Lead.
2) Validate impacted components/regions using the component health dashboards.
3) Publish first Statuspage update within TTFU target (minimal + accurate).
4) Post the exact published text into the incident channel.
5) Coordinate with Support/CS on messaging alignment.
6) On major changes (scope, mitigation, recovery), publish an update.
7) Prior to “Resolved”, confirm:
   * metrics are stable (anti-flap guidance)
   * IC agrees incident is mitigated
   * approval obtained if Sev0/Sev1
8) Ensure the post-incident summary draft is created within the policy window.

h1. Exceptions and escalation
If you cannot staff Comms Lead due to on-call constraints:
* IC escalates to SRE on-call manager or SRE leadership delegate to assign a CL.
* If no CL is available, IC must either:
  * publish the minimal update themselves, or
  * explicitly delegate public comms to Support/CS lead (temporary) and document it in the channel.

h1. Change log
* 2025-11-26: Clarified approval checkpoints for Sev0/Sev1 and Dedicated/Private public visibility guidance.
* 2025-11-18: Added TTFU targets and update frequency guidelines.
* 2025-11-12: Initial publication (Comms Lead role + coordination loop).

title: Game Day Plan: Hosted Overload Protection (Circuit Breakers + Load Shedding)
preview:
## Summary
This game day validates Redwood Hosted API overload protection behaviors end-to-end: gateway admission control, policy evaluation, smart routing fallback, and serving runtime backpressure. The focus is safe degradation under stress (reject early, stable error semantics, avoid retry storms) while protecting tenant isolation and keeping p95/p99 latency within guardrails.

## Objectives
1) Confirm overload protection prevents overload cascades (tail latency + timeouts) during bursty demand.
2) Validate customer-facing error semantics (HTTP mapping, error codes, Retry-After, correlation IDs).
3) Verify dependency brownouts do not amplify into platform-wide incidents (circuit breaker trip + fast fail).
4) Validate regional capacity dips are handled predictably (shed + optional fallback routing) without loops.
5) Exercise oncall operational muscle: detection, triage, mitigations, rollback, comms, and post-event data capture.

## In-scope systems
- Hosted API Gateway: admission middleware (token bucket / concurrency caps), breaker evaluation, Retry-After
- Overload Policy Engine: tier defaults + per-tenant/per-route/model overrides
- Smart Routing: fallback to compatible model/region (when configured), loop prevention
- Serving Runtime Scheduling: queue depth/saturation signals, backpressure, feature reduction flags
- Observability: dashboards/alerts, incident-bot breaker state announcements

## Out of scope
- Billing/credits policy changes (beyond existing usage enforcement hooks)
- Multi-provider capacity brokering beyond existing routing primitives
- SDK API redesign (we validate server-side semantics; SDK behavior is informational)

## Preconditions (must be green before starting)
- Canary region selected and isolated from customer traffic (or use internal-only tenant allowlist).
- All participants have access to:
  - Overload dashboards (shed rate, breaker open rate, runtime saturation)
  - Gateway logs and correlation IDs
  - Routing decision logs (fallback attempts + reasons)
  - Feature flag console for overload policies
- Error taxonomy implemented and deployed in the environment under test (ADR-014).
- Incident-bot configured to announce breaker state changes in the game day channel.
- Load generator ready (perf-canary scenario or internal harness) with ability to vary:
  - Concurrency
  - Prompt lengths
  - Streaming vs non-streaming
  - Tool-calling enabled/disabled
- Rollback plan reviewed and executable (single-step flag disable + deployment rollback if needed).

## Success criteria (pass/fail)
### Customer experience (API-level)
- Requests are rejected early with stable, documented overload errors (not timeouts and not ambiguous 5xx).
- Overload responses include:
  - A stable overload error code
  - A correlation/request ID
  - Retry guidance (Retry-After header present where applicable)
- No retry storms observed (no sustained RPS amplification after clients respect Retry-After during the test).

### Reliability / SLO protection
- Under burst load:
  - p99 latency does not enter runaway growth for >5 minutes
  - 5xx/timeouts are lower than baseline “no shedding” control test (where available)
  - Error budget burn attributable to timeouts is reduced; overload rejections become the dominant failure mode (by design)
- Noisy-neighbor protection:
  - A single “burst tenant” does not degrade other tenants beyond agreed guardrails

### System correctness
- Breakers open/close with expected hysteresis (no flapping).
- Routing fallback (when configured) respects:
  - Bounded retries
  - Loop prevention
  - Compatibility constraints (only allowed model variants)
- Runtime saturation metrics align with actual behavior (queue depth increases precede shedding; metrics are not misleading).

### Operational readiness
- Oncall follows the runbook successfully:
  - Detect → verify → mitigate → communicate → recover → capture data
- Comms templates are usable; a short internal status update is published within 10 minutes of scenario start.

## Roles and responsibilities
### Game day leads
- Facilitator / Timekeeper: Talia Benson
- Technical lead (SRE): Rafael Mendes

### Incident roles (simulated)
- Incident Commander (IC): Christine Wong
  - Owns decision-making, declares incident state, coordinates responders
- Operations / Mitigation lead: Ethan Park
  - Executes admission/policy changes, confirms impact, manages rollback
- Routing lead: Mina Haddad
  - Validates fallback behavior, routing logs, loop prevention
- Runtime lead: Sofia Ivanova
  - Interprets saturation signals, runtime backpressure, batching caps
- Observability lead: Aisha Bello
  - Confirms dashboards/alerts, captures metrics snapshots, notes gaps
- Comms lead: Nikhil Sharma
  - Drafts internal updates; ensures customer-facing semantics align with product expectations
- Scribe: Connor O'Brien
  - Captures timeline, decisions, action items, and “unknowns”

### Communication channels
- Primary coordination: #game-day-overload (Slack)
- Escalation (if needed): #eng-oncall, #incidents
- War room: Zoom link posted in #game-day-overload at start

## Run of show (90–120 minutes)
1) 0:00–0:10 — Kickoff
   - Confirm preconditions, roles, and safety boundaries
   - Confirm rollback switches and who can execute them
2) 0:10–0:25 — Baseline verification
   - Validate dashboards are live and show normal levels
   - Run a 5-minute “steady state” load to confirm metrics
3) 0:25–0:55 — Scenario 1: Burst traffic (noisy-neighbor)
4) 0:55–1:25 — Scenario 2: Dependency brownout
5) 1:25–1:55 — Scenario 3: Regional capacity dip + fallback
6) 1:55–2:10 — Recovery verification
   - Ensure breakers close, shed rate returns to ~0, latency normalizes
7) 2:10–2:30 — Debrief
   - Review pass/fail criteria and immediate follow-ups

## Scenario 1: Burst traffic (noisy-neighbor / prompt variance)
### Purpose
Validate that admission control + shedding protect tail latency and other tenants when one tenant spikes concurrency and prompt size.

### Setup
- Target region: us-east (canary)
- Tenants:
  - Tenant A (burst): internal test tenant with elevated limits
  - Tenant B (control): internal steady tenant at constant low QPS
- Routes:
  - /v1/chat.completions (streaming on)
  - /v1/responses (non-streaming)
- Models: one primary model + one compatible fallback model (configured but not required for this scenario)

### Inject
- Ramp Tenant A load over 10 minutes:
  - Increase concurrency 5x
  - Increase prompt size distribution (p50 ~2k tokens, p99 ~16k tokens)
  - Enable streaming for 60% of requests

### Expected outcomes
- Breaker transitions:
  - Soft shedding begins as runtime queue depth rises
  - Breaker opens only if thresholds are exceeded (avoid premature opening)
- API behavior:
  - Shed requests return overload errors with correct taxonomy and Retry-After
  - Correlation IDs present and traceable across gateway/routing/runtime logs
- Tenant isolation:
  - Tenant B latency degradation is bounded and recovers quickly

### Data to capture
- Time series screenshots:
  - Shed rate by tenant/route
  - Breaker open rate by region
  - Runtime queue depth and token/sec saturation
  - p95/p99 latency by tenant
- Sample of 10 overload responses (raw HTTP) verifying:
  - status code mapping (e.g., 429 vs 503 as configured)
  - error code
  - Retry-After presence and format
  - correlation ID

### Failure conditions / stop criteria
- Sustained p99 > agreed guardrail for >5 minutes with no shedding
- Breaker flapping (open/close cycling >3 times within 10 minutes)
- Tenant B experiences widespread timeouts or elevated 5xx unrelated to shedding

## Scenario 2: Dependency brownout (downstream health degradation)
### Purpose
Validate that partial dependency failures (e.g., auth/token verification latency, policy engine lag, model registry lookups) don’t cause request pileups and retry storms.

### Setup
- Select one dependency to brownout in the canary environment (choose one):
  - Policy engine evaluation latency injection
  - Routing metadata store latency injection
  - Upstream auth verification latency injection

### Inject
- Introduce 500–1500ms latency + 5–10% errors for the selected dependency for 10 minutes.

### Expected outcomes
- Gateway behavior:
  - Requests fail fast with a documented error where appropriate
  - Overload protection does not misclassify dependency errors as runtime saturation unless signals indicate overload
- Breaker behavior:
  - Dependency-related circuit breaker (if configured) opens and reduces request fanout
  - Retry-After is used to prevent client amplification

### Data to capture
- Dependency latency/error graphs
- Admission outcomes breakdown (accepted vs shed vs dependency-failed)
- Evidence of no queue buildup:
  - Scheduler backlog does not grow unbounded
  - No runaway open connections

### Failure conditions / stop criteria
- Significant increase in timeouts (client-side) instead of controlled errors
- Requests stuck waiting on dependency (p99 keeps climbing with no protective response)

## Scenario 3: Regional capacity dip + fallback routing
### Purpose
Validate that capacity dips in one region trigger controlled shedding and (when configured) safe routing fallback without loops or excessive retries.

### Setup
- Primary region: us-east
- Secondary region: us-west (healthy)
- Fallback policy enabled for an internal tenant:
  - If breaker open in us-east, route to us-west for compatible models only
  - Max fallback attempts: 1 (bounded)

### Inject (choose one)
- Reduce available GPU capacity in us-east canary pool (simulate autoscaler lag / node drain)
OR
- Force runtime saturation signal to exceed threshold (controlled test flag)

### Expected outcomes
- Routing behavior:
  - A subset of requests are rerouted to us-west once us-east breaker is open
  - Loop prevention works (no bouncing between regions)
- Customer experience:
  - Requests either succeed via fallback or fail fast with overload semantics
  - No ambiguous timeouts during the transition

### Data to capture
- Routing decision logs (fallback attempted, fallback succeeded/failed, reason codes)
- Regional latency and success rates
- Overload response samples when fallback is not possible

### Failure conditions / stop criteria
- Evidence of fallback loops or >1 retry per request
- Cross-region overload propagation (us-west begins shedding due to unbounded spillover)

## Operational actions allowed during the game day (safe toggles)
- Enable/disable overload policy flags for the internal tenant allowlist
- Adjust admission caps within pre-approved bounds:
  - Lower max concurrent streaming sessions
  - Reduce max_tokens cap (internal-only) as a feature reduction test
- Force breaker open/close in a controlled way (test-only) to validate end-to-end wiring

## Actions NOT allowed (safety boundaries)
- No production customer traffic impact (unless explicitly approved via change management)
- No schema changes, no deploys to shared production during the game day window
- No disabling of core auth/security controls

## Comms templates (internal)
### Initial message (within 10 minutes)
“[GAME DAY] Starting overload protection simulation in <region>. Expect increased overload errors for internal test tenants only. Tracking in <channel>. IC: <name>.”

### Mid-scenario update
“[GAME DAY] Scenario <#> running. Observed: <key metrics>. Actions taken: <toggles>. Next checkpoint in 10 min.”

### Completion message
“[GAME DAY] Completed. Summary: <pass/fail per scenario>. Follow-ups: <top 3 actions>. Debrief doc: <link>.”

## Post-game day deliverables
- Timeline + decisions + metric snapshots (scribe)
- List of bugs/issues and owners (Linear tickets)
- Updates needed to:
  - Hosted overload circuit breaker runbook
  - Overload dashboards/alerts spec
  - Docs/support guidance on Retry-After and error codes

## Appendix: Checklist
- [ ] Roles assigned and confirmed
- [ ] Dashboards green and accessible
- [ ] Load generator ready and validated
- [ ] Breaker state announcements visible in Slack
- [ ] Rollback switch tested (no-op dry run)
- [ ] Data capture plan assigned (who takes which screenshots/samples)

---
Owner: Eng SRE (Rafael Mendes). For questions or changes to scenario scope, coordinate in #game-day-overload and update the related runbooks/ADRs if semantics change.

title: Status Page Component Taxonomy (Public) — Component List, Definitions, Ownership, and Mapping Rules
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s canonical **public** status page component taxonomy. It is the shared reference for:
- Which components exist on the public status page (names, IDs, grouping).
- What each component means (scope, common failure modes, customer interpretation).
- Ownership (technical owner for mitigation + communications owner for updates).
- **Mapping rules** from internal services/signals to public components using the standard axes: **product area × region × capability**.

This taxonomy is designed to improve customer clarity without exposing customer-specific capacity, topology, or sensitive details.

---

## What this taxonomy is (and is not)
### In scope
- Hosted API components split by **region** and **capability** where failure modes differ (e.g., Streaming vs non-streaming).
- Console components (read-only surfaces that can fail independently from inference).
- Dedicated components limited to **shared/control-plane signals** that affect multiple customers.
- Mapping guidance and examples for on-call, ICs, Comms Leads, and incident-bot automation.

### Out of scope / non-goals
- Customer-specific Dedicated dataplane health (handled via private comms, not public components).
- Publishing internal service names, customer names, or capacity constraints.
- Promise language that implies a specific SLA.

---

## Source of truth and change management
- **Source of truth:** The public component registry and service→component mapping live in versioned config (see ADR-014). This Confluence page is the human-readable specification.
- **Change control:**
  1) Propose changes via PR to the registry/mapping.
  2) Review required: SRE + Observability + (as needed) Console PM and Support.
  3) Validate in staging status page + incident-bot dry run.
  4) Roll out behind a feature flag and update the public status page layout grouping.

---

## Axes and naming conventions
### Axes
1) **Product area**: Hosted API, Console, Dedicated (control-plane only), etc.
2) **Region**: the customer-facing region where the request is served.
3) **Capability**: user-visible feature with distinct failure modes/signals.

### Canonical regions (public)
We expose a constrained set of regions to keep the status page understandable.
- **us-east** (N. Virginia)
- **us-west** (Oregon)
- **eu-central** (Frankfurt)
- **eu-west** (Ireland)
- **ap-southeast** (Singapore)

Notes:
- If internal sub-regions/AZs differ, we still roll up to the canonical region for public reporting.
- If a problem affects multiple regions, list all impacted regions explicitly (avoid “Global” unless truly global).

### Component naming
Pattern: **{Product Area} — {Capability} ({Region})**
Examples:
- Hosted API — Chat Completions (us-east)
- Hosted API — Streaming (eu-west)

For components that are not region-scoped (e.g., Console UI), omit region.

### Status semantics (customer-facing)
- **Operational:** within expected error/latency bands.
- **Degraded Performance:** elevated error rates and/or latency beyond thresholds for a sustained period.
- **Partial Outage:** significant functional impact for a subset of requests/users (e.g., one capability in one region).
- **Major Outage:** broad inability to serve requests for a component.

Exact thresholds and anti-flap behavior are defined in the customer-facing status signals standard; on-call should not “flip” a component based on a single spike.

---

## Canonical component list (public)
The table below is the canonical list. The **component_id** is stable and used by incident-bot, dashboards, and the mapping registry.

### A) Hosted API (regional components)
These represent inference request-serving from Redwood Hosted.

| component_id | Public display name | Scope / definition | Capability | Region-scoped | Primary technical owner | Comms stakeholders | Common failure modes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| hosted.chat.us-east | Hosted API — Chat Completions (us-east) | Chat/completions style generation endpoints excluding streaming transport issues. | chat | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | 5xx spikes, latency regression, routing misconfig, model load failures |
| hosted.chat.us-west | Hosted API — Chat Completions (us-west) | Same as above for us-west. | chat | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.chat.eu-central | Hosted API — Chat Completions (eu-central) | Same as above for eu-central. | chat | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.chat.eu-west | Hosted API — Chat Completions (eu-west) | Same as above for eu-west. | chat | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.chat.ap-southeast | Hosted API — Chat Completions (ap-southeast) | Same as above for ap-southeast. | chat | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.streaming.us-east | Hosted API — Streaming (us-east) | Streaming transport/connection behavior for streaming responses (disconnects, stalled streams). | streaming | Yes | Runtime / Infra | SRE, Support | Disconnect spikes, stream stalls, proxy buffering regressions |
| hosted.streaming.us-west | Hosted API — Streaming (us-west) | Same as above for us-west. | streaming | Yes | Runtime / Infra | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.streaming.eu-central | Hosted API — Streaming (eu-central) | Same as above for eu-central. | streaming | Yes | Runtime / Infra | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.streaming.eu-west | Hosted API — Streaming (eu-west) | Same as above for eu-west. | streaming | Yes | Runtime / Infra | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.streaming.ap-southeast | Hosted API — Streaming (ap-southeast) | Same as above for ap-southeast. | streaming | Yes | Runtime / Infra | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.embeddings.us-east | Hosted API — Embeddings (us-east) | Embeddings endpoint behavior and embedding job execution; excludes Console. | embeddings | Yes | Infra / Observability | SRE, Support | Elevated 4xx/5xx, queue/scheduler pressure, model loading |
| hosted.embeddings.us-west | Hosted API — Embeddings (us-west) | Same as above for us-west. | embeddings | Yes | Infra / Observability | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.embeddings.eu-central | Hosted API — Embeddings (eu-central) | Same as above for eu-central. | embeddings | Yes | Infra / Observability | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.embeddings.eu-west | Hosted API — Embeddings (eu-west) | Same as above for eu-west. | embeddings | Yes | Infra / Observability | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.embeddings.ap-southeast | Hosted API — Embeddings (ap-southeast) | Same as above for ap-southeast. | embeddings | Yes | Infra / Observability | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.rerank.us-east | Hosted API — Reranking (us-east) | Reranking endpoint behavior (scoring/ranking requests). | reranking | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Elevated error %, scoring timeouts |
| hosted.rerank.us-west | Hosted API — Reranking (us-west) | Same as above for us-west. | reranking | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.rerank.eu-central | Hosted API — Reranking (eu-central) | Same as above for eu-central. | reranking | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.rerank.eu-west | Hosted API — Reranking (eu-west) | Same as above for eu-west. | reranking | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |
| hosted.rerank.ap-southeast | Hosted API — Reranking (ap-southeast) | Same as above for ap-southeast. | reranking | Yes | Infra / Runtime | SRE, Support | Same as above |

Notes:
- We intentionally separate **Streaming** from Chat Completions because it fails differently (disconnects/stalls) and creates customer confusion when grouped.
- Embeddings and Reranking are split to address recurring Support escalations where “API operational” masked non-chat failures.

### B) Console (global components)
Console is customer-facing but is not in the inference dataplane; it can degrade independently.

| component_id | Public display name | Scope / definition | Region-scoped | Primary technical owner | Comms stakeholders | Common failure modes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| console.web | Console — Web UI | Console UI access and core navigation. Includes auth redirects as experienced within Console. | No | Product (Console) | SRE, Support | 5xx from console backend, broken deploy, auth redirect loop |
| console.api | Console — API / Backend | Backend used by Console for usage dashboards, incident links, and configuration surfaces. | No | Product (Console) | SRE, Support | Backend timeouts, data pipeline lag, permission errors |

### C) Dedicated (public components limited to shared signals)
Dedicated is primarily a customer-specific deployment. Public reporting is limited to shared services that impact multiple Dedicated customers.

| component_id | Public display name | Scope / definition | Region-scoped | Primary technical owner | Comms stakeholders | Common failure modes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| dedicated.control-plane | Dedicated — Control Plane | Dedicated provisioning, scaling actions, and control-plane APIs that may impact multiple Dedicated environments. | No | Infra | SRE, Support, CS | Provisioning delays, control-plane API errors, rollout/upgrade pipeline issues |

Rules:
- Do **not** mark public Dedicated components for a single-customer dataplane issue. Use private customer comms and CS escalation procedures.
- If a control-plane incident affects a subset of regions, the incident update text must state “some Dedicated customers” and avoid listing customer names.

### D) Status Page / Communications surfaces
These are listed only if we explicitly choose to expose them publicly (optional). If not exposed, they remain internal-only.

| component_id | Public display name | Scope / definition | Region-scoped | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| comms.statuspage | Status Page Publishing | Ability to publish and display accurate status updates. | No | SRE |

---

## Ownership model (how to route questions and approvals)
For each component:
- **Technical owner:** who leads mitigation and verifies impact (typically Infra, Runtime, or Console).
- **Comms stakeholders:** who must be looped for customer-facing updates (SRE on-call; Support/CS for high-severity).

Default routing:
- Hosted API components → **SRE on-call** (IC) + Infra/Runtime on-call for mitigation.
- Console components → Console on-call/owner (Product Eng) + SRE for incident coordination.
- Dedicated control-plane → Infra on-call + SRE for comms discipline + CS for affected enterprise accounts.

Approval reminder:
- For Sev0/Sev1, follow the incident comms roles & responsibilities page for the approval chain and the optional Support approval step.

---

## Mapping rules (internal signals → public components)
### 1) Choose the narrowest truthful component(s)
When posting an incident or setting a component state:
- Prefer **capability-specific** components (e.g., Streaming) over broader ones (e.g., Chat) when signals indicate a capability-specific failure.
- Prefer **region-scoped** components when impact is limited to one region.
- If multiple components are impacted, list each affected component explicitly.

Avoid:
- Marking all Hosted API regions degraded “just in case.”
- Using a broader component to represent a narrower issue (e.g., Chat to represent Embeddings failures).

### 2) Region selection rules
- A region is impacted if its customer-facing SLO signals for the component breach thresholds for the sustained window (see signals standard).
- If only a subset of traffic is impacted due to routing, still mark the region component impacted if customers in that region experience errors/latency beyond thresholds.
- If region attribution is unknown, use the best-known evidence (gateway tags, router logs, dashboards). If still unclear, post an Investigating update with the suspected region(s) and correct quickly once verified.

### 3) Capability classification rules
Use the following capability mapping based on the primary customer-observed failure:
- **streaming**: disconnects, stalled streams, partial token streaming failures, streaming-specific 499/504 patterns.
- **chat**: non-streaming request failures/latency for chat/completions style endpoints.
- **embeddings**: embedding creation failures/latency; includes batch embedding endpoints if customer-facing.
- **reranking**: reranking/scoring endpoints.

If a single underlying issue impacts multiple capabilities (e.g., GPU scheduler outage), mark each capability component that is measurably impacted.

### 4) “Degraded” vs “Partial Outage” vs “Major Outage”
Use customer meaning, not internal severity:
- **Degraded Performance**: latency elevated or error rate moderately elevated; most requests still succeed.
- **Partial Outage**: a material portion of requests fail, or a major feature subset is unavailable.
- **Major Outage**: most requests fail for that component/region.

When in doubt early:
- Start with Degraded Performance if signal shows impact but scope is uncertain.
- Update to Partial/Major once scope is confirmed.

### 5) Anti-flap and hysteresis (posting discipline)
- Do not set a component to Degraded/Outage based on <10 minutes of transient breach unless customer impact is clearly ongoing.
- Do not set a component back to Operational until recovery criteria is met (typically sustained recovery window + no active mitigation rollbacks pending).
- Incident-bot may suggest status changes; the IC/Comms Lead must verify with dashboards before publishing.

### 6) What we never publish in component naming or descriptions
- Customer names, tenant identifiers, or “only Customer X was affected.”
- Specific capacity constraints (“we ran out of GPUs for customer Y”).
- Sensitive infrastructure details (AZ identifiers, vendor incident IDs not already public).

---

## Service-to-component mapping guidance (implementation-level)
This section provides guidance for the mapping layer and tagging; detailed mapping lives in the registry config.

### Required tags for accurate mapping
Metrics and traces that drive component health should include:
- **region**: one of the canonical regions
- **capability**: chat | streaming | embeddings | reranking
- **component_id**: stable ID from this taxonomy (emitted by gateways where possible)
- **route_family** (optional): used to disambiguate endpoints when capability is ambiguous

Primary signal sources:
- API gateway metrics (request rate, error %, latency p95/p99)
- Streaming-specific disconnect and stall counters
- Scheduler/queue health metrics (used as supporting evidence; not alone sufficient for public status)

### Mapping precedence
When multiple sources disagree:
1) Prefer **customer-observed** signals (gateway + streaming disconnects).
2) Use internal dependency signals as confirmation (scheduler saturation, model load failures).
3) If attribution is ambiguous, keep status scoped to the narrowest confirmed set and update frequently.

---

## Examples (how to pick components)
### Example 1: Streaming disconnect spike only in us-east
Signals:
- hosted.streaming.us-east disconnect rate breaches threshold for 15 minutes
- hosted.chat.us-east error rate stable
Action:
- Mark **Hosted API — Streaming (us-east)** as Degraded Performance.
- Do not mark Chat Completions as impacted.

### Example 2: Embeddings failing while Chat is operational in eu-west
Signals:
- hosted.embeddings.eu-west 5xx elevated
- hosted.chat.eu-west OK
Action:
- Mark **Hosted API — Embeddings (eu-west)** as Partial Outage.
- Keep Chat Completions operational.

### Example 3: Console dashboards not loading, API serving fine
Signals:
- Console API backend 5xx elevated
- Hosted components normal
Action:
- Mark **Console — API / Backend** as Degraded/Partial Outage depending on impact.
- No Hosted API components impacted.

### Example 4: Dedicated provisioning delays across multiple customers
Signals:
- Provisioning workflow timeouts and increased failure rate
- Multiple Dedicated environments unable to scale
Action:
- Mark **Dedicated — Control Plane** as Degraded Performance.
- Comms must avoid customer specifics; Support/CS to handle account-specific follow-ups.

---

## Rollout and audit expectations
- On-call and Comms Leads should use the status-page update runbook for posting steps and verification.
- Weekly internal audits compare incident tags to component updates to detect mismatches (e.g., embeddings errors posted as chat).
- If you find a taxonomy gap (missing component or confusing definition), file a request in the status transparency project queue and propose a PR to the registry.

---

## Quick reference (component ID cheatsheet)
Hosted API (by capability):
- chat: hosted.chat.{region}
- streaming: hosted.streaming.{region}
- embeddings: hosted.embeddings.{region}
- reranking: hosted.rerank.{region}

Console:
- console.web
- console.api

Dedicated:
- dedicated.control-plane

- **route_family** (optional): used to disambiguate endpoints when capability is ambiguous

Primary signal sources:
- API gateway metrics (request rate, error %, latency p95/p99)
- Streaming-specific disconnect and stall counters
- Scheduler/queue health metrics (used as supporting evidence; not alone sufficient for public status)

### Mapping precedence
When multiple sources disagree:
1) Prefer **customer-observed** signals (gateway + streaming disconnects).
2) Use internal dependency signals as confirmation (scheduler saturation, model load failures).
3) If attribution is ambiguous, keep status scoped to the narrowest confirmed set and update frequently.

---

## Examples (how to pick components)
### Example 1: Streaming disconnect spike only in us-east
Signals:
- hosted.streaming.us-east disconnect rate breaches threshold for 15 minutes
- hosted.chat.us-east error rate stable
Action:
- Mark **Hosted API — Streaming (us-east)** as Degraded Performance.
- Do not mark Chat Completions as impacted.

### Example 2: Embeddings failing while Chat is operational in eu-west
Signals:
- hosted.embeddings.eu-west 5xx elevated
- hosted.chat.eu-west OK
Action:
- Mark **Hosted API — Embeddings (eu-west)** as Partial Outage.
- Keep Chat Completions operational.

### Example 3: Console dashboards not loading, API serving fine
Signals:
- Console API backend 5xx elevated
- Hosted components normal
Action:
- Mark **Console — API / Backend** as Degraded/Partial Outage depending on impact.
- No Hosted API components impacted.

### Example 4: Dedicated provisioning delays across multiple customers
Signals:
- Provisioning workflow timeouts and increased failure rate
- Multiple Dedicated environments unable to scale
Action:
- Mark **Dedicated — Control Plane** as Degraded Performance.
- Comms must avoid customer specifics; Support/CS to handle account-specific follow-ups.

---

## Rollout and audit expectations
- On-call and Comms Leads should use the status-page update runbook for posting steps and verification.
- Weekly internal audits compare incident tags to component updates to detect mismatches (e.g., embeddings errors posted as chat).
- If you find a taxonomy gap (missing component or confusing definition), file a request in the status transparency project queue and propose a PR to the registry.

---

## Quick reference (component ID cheatsheet)
Hosted API (by capability):
- chat: hosted.chat.{region}
- streaming: hosted.streaming.{region}
- embeddings: hosted.embeddings.{region}
- reranking: hosted.rerank.{region}

Console:
- console.web
- console.api

Dedicated:
- dedicated.control-plane

title: Postmortem: 2025-11-07 US-East Streaming Degradation
preview:
## Summary
On 2025-11-07, customers using **streaming responses** in **US-East** experienced elevated disconnects and intermittent upstream errors. Non-streaming requests were largely unaffected.

This incident exposed a major gap in our customer-facing communication: the public status page component coverage was too coarse ("Hosted API" only), which made it difficult to accurately reflect a streaming-only regional degradation. The resulting delay and ambiguity in external updates directly contributed to customer confusion and escalations.

**Severity:** Sev1

## Customer impact
**Who was affected**
- Customers using streaming (SSE) for Chat Completions via the Hosted API in **us-east-1**.
- Impact was most visible for long-lived streams (longer generations / higher token counts).

**What customers experienced**
- Increased streaming disconnects (client-visible mid-stream termination).
- Intermittent 502/504 errors at stream setup.
- Retries sometimes succeeded; some clients fell back to non-streaming where implemented.

**Quantified impact (approx.)**
- Peak streaming disconnect rate in us-east-1: **~8–12%** (baseline ~0.2–0.5%).
- Error spikes concentrated on `POST /v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true`.

**Not impacted / minimally impacted**
- Embeddings and reranking endpoints.
- Non-streaming chat completions in us-east-1 (slight latency increase observed, within internal alert thresholds).

**Customer reports / escalations**
- Support received multiple tickets reporting “status page green but streaming failing.”
- Notable follow-ups: SUP-28436 (status accuracy dispute), SUP-28549 (streaming disconnects not reflected).

## Incident timeline (UTC)
> Note: times reconstructed from PagerDuty + #incidents thread + deploy logs.

| Time (UTC) | Event |
|---|---|
| 17:12 | First measurable increase in streaming disconnects in us-east-1. |
| 17:18 | Automated alert fired: elevated `stream_disconnect_rate` (internal) for us-east streaming edge. Alert did not map to a customer-facing component. |
| 17:23 | Incident declared Sev1; IC assigned. Initial focus: validate whether this is broader API or streaming-only. |
| 17:31 | Infra identified correlation with a config rollout on the streaming edge pool in us-east-1 (connection handling change). |
| 17:38 | Mitigation started: rollback of streaming edge config + drain/recycle affected nodes. |
| 17:45 | Disconnect rate begins to fall but remains elevated; continued recycling. |
| 17:56 | Service stabilizing; disconnect rate trending toward baseline. |
| 18:06 | Metrics back to baseline; monitoring period begins. |
| 18:10 | First public status page update posted (late relative to customer impact window). |
| 18:32 | Status page updated to “Monitoring” then “Resolved.” |

## Root cause
A **configuration change** rolled out to the **us-east streaming edge pool** altered connection/timeout behavior under sustained streaming load. The new settings increased the likelihood of premature upstream resets for long-lived connections, resulting in elevated disconnects.

**Root cause class:** change-related regression (config rollout)

**Why only streaming was affected**
- Streaming requests maintain long-lived connections and are more sensitive to keepalive/idle-timeout and buffering behaviors.
- Non-streaming requests complete quickly and did not cross the problematic thresholds at meaningful rates.

## Detection and response
**Detection**
- We had internal signal coverage (stream disconnect rate by region) that detected the issue quickly.
- However, the signal was not integrated into a customer-facing status component model, leading to uncertainty about what to mark publicly.

**Response**
- Infra rolled back the config and recycled the affected pool; results were immediate but required multiple cycles to fully stabilize.

## Communications review (main learning)
### What we did externally
- Public status page update was posted after mitigation had largely succeeded.
- The incident was described generically as “API degradation in US-East,” which was not precise and did not help customers determine whether streaming specifically was affected.

### What went wrong
1) **Status page component taxonomy was too coarse**
   - Only “Hosted API” existed as a top-level component; no streaming capability component, and no region-scoped components.
   - Team debated whether to mark “Hosted API” as degraded (risk: over-broad, causes unnecessary alarm) vs leaving it green (risk: inaccurate for impacted customers). This ambiguity delayed publication.

2) **Lack of standardized incident update templates**
   - Drafting the first update took too long, and wording was inconsistent across channels (#incidents vs Support macros).
   - The update did not clearly state symptoms (“streaming disconnects”) and affected surface (“stream=true”).

3) **Unclear ownership/role for comms during the incident**
   - No dedicated Comms Lead role was assigned early.
   - Approval expectations were unclear (IC vs Support lead vs SRE lead), increasing hesitation to post.

4) **Status accuracy disputes were predictable but we weren’t ready**
   - Support had limited tooling to point to component-scoped evidence (dashboards) and had to escalate manually.

### What went well
- Internal detection was fast.
- Technical mitigation (rollback + recycle) was effective and low-risk.
- Support surfaced customer impact details early, which helped validate streaming-only scope.

### What didn’t go well
- Time to first customer-facing update (TTFU) was too slow relative to impact.
- Public status page did not reflect the incident during the customer impact window.
- Messaging did not align to a stable component model (product surface + region + capability), creating confusion and extra support load.

## Contributing factors
- **Change management:** streaming-edge config rollout proceeded without a streaming-specific canary health gate.
- **Observability-to-comms gap:** we had internal streaming signals, but no mapping to status page components.
- **Process gap:** lack of a lightweight, rehearsed workflow for “partial regional + capability-only” incidents.

## Corrective actions / follow-ups
### Immediate fixes (completed)
- Rolled back the streaming edge config change in us-east-1.
- Added a temporary guardrail to block re-applying the settings without explicit SRE + Infra review.

### Prevent recurrence (technical)
1) **Streaming-edge rollout guardrails**
   - Add a canary gate for streaming disconnect rate and upstream reset rate (per region) before full rollout.
   - Owner: Irene Choi
   - Target: 2025-12-06

2) **Region-aware streaming metrics and dashboards**
   - Ensure `stream_disconnect_rate`, `upstream_reset_rate`, and stream setup error rates are tagged by region and surfaced on a standard dashboard.
   - Owner: Allison Grant
   - Target: 2025-12-13

### Prevent recurrence (customer-facing transparency) — feeds into “Status page transparency upgrades”
These items are explicitly in scope for the cross-functional initiative to improve status page granularity, standardize communications, and speed publishable summaries.

1) **Add streaming as a first-class public status page capability component (by region)**
   - Proposed components (example):
     - Hosted API — Chat (Non-streaming) — US-East
     - Hosted API — Chat Streaming — US-East
   - Goal: allow accurate marking of streaming-only incidents without flagging the entire Hosted API.
   - Owner: Christine Wong + Mina Haddad
   - Target: Phase 1 of Status Page Transparency Upgrades

2) **Define and adopt customer-facing status signals for streaming**
   - Standardize thresholds for “Degraded Performance” vs “Partial Outage” based on disconnect rate + setup errors.
   - Include anti-flap guidance (hysteresis + minimum duration).
   - Owner: Allison Grant
   - Target: Phase 1–2

3) **Incident comms templates + Comms Lead role**
   - Adopt templated updates for Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved.
   - Assign Comms Lead at incident start; set TTFU target and reminders.
   - Owner: Rafael Mendes + Dev Patel
   - Target: Phase 2

4) **Support escalation playbook for status accuracy disputes**
   - Provide a standard response and evidence checklist (dashboard links per component/region).
   - Owner: Dev Patel
   - Target: Phase 2

5) **Publishable post-incident summary format (fast, safe, consistent)**
   - Create a structured summary template suitable for external publication without leaking customer-specific capacity data.
   - Owner: Elliot Price + Michael Grant (Legal review)
   - Target: Phase 2–3

## Action items (tracking)
| ID | Action | Owner | Priority | Target date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AI-1 | Add streaming capability components by region to status taxonomy | Christine Wong / Mina Haddad | P0 | 2025-12-20 |
| AI-2 | Define streaming customer-facing status signals (thresholds + anti-flap) | Allison Grant | P0 | 2025-12-20 |
| AI-3 | Add Comms Lead role + TTFU guidance to incident process | Rafael Mendes | P0 | 2025-12-06 |
| AI-4 | Standardize incident update templates and Support macros | Dev Patel / Wei Chen | P1 | 2025-12-13 |
| AI-5 | Add streaming-edge rollout gate (disconnect/reset health) | Irene Choi | P1 | 2025-12-06 |
| AI-6 | Create status accuracy dispute escalation checklist + dashboard links | Dev Patel | P1 | 2025-12-13 |

## Appendix
### Internal references
- #incidents thread: 1760048421 (streaming-only regional impact discussion)
- #incidents thread: 1760049133 (request for comms template; wording iteration)

### Notes on confidentiality
- Do not include customer names, dedicated capacity details, or customer-specific traffic patterns in any public summary.
- When discussing mitigations publicly, describe the class of change (e.g., “rolled back a configuration change affecting streaming connections in US-East”) without exposing infrastructure topology details.
